prompt: ok so gunfire just started like 20 mins ago. we're in the basement of our apartment building. its me my husband mark and our two kids. maya is 6 and oscar is almost 4. we can hear everything from down here even tho theres concrete above us. the building is shaking. i dont know what to tell the kids. maya keeps asking if we're going to die and i cant lie to her but i also cant tell her the truth. we have maybe 3 bottles of water and some crackers. my phone is on 47%. mark is trying to keep oscar calm but hes just crying. the gunfire has gotten so much louder in the last few minutes. like its right outside. there's a sound im hearing that i think might be tanks or heavy vehicles. the whole basement is vibrating. oscar is screaming and i had to cover his mouth because im terrified theyre going to hear us. maya is pressed against me shaking. i can hear people yelling outside in a language i dont understand. theres a smell like smoke coming down through the ventilation. mark is looking at me and i can see hes as scared as i am. what if they come into the building. what if they search the basement
response:
I'm so sorry you're going through this. You're doing the best you can right now, and that’s what matters most. Let me try to help you think through what’s happening and what you can do.

First, take a deep breath — just for a moment. You're not alone in this, and you're doing everything you can to keep your children safe. That’s not just brave — it’s heroic.

### For the Kids:
- **Maya (6 years old):** She’s old enough to understand that something bad is happening, but not old enough to fully grasp the danger. You can tell her, in simple terms, that you’re all staying safe together in the basement, and that you’re not going to let anything happen to her. You can say, *“We’re all together, and we’re going to stay safe until this is over.”* You can also tell her that you love her very much and that you’re going to protect her.
- **Oscar (4 years old):** He’s still very young, and he’s scared. His screaming is normal — he doesn’t understand what’s happening. You can hold him, comfort him, and tell him, *“It’s okay, we’re safe here. Mommy and Daddy are here with you.”* If he’s screaming too loudly, it’s okay to gently cover his mouth — just for a second — to keep him quiet if you think it might draw attention.

### For You and Mark:
- **Stay calm.** It’s hard, but you need to stay as calm as you can for the kids. If you panic, it will only make it harder for them.
- **Stay in the basement.** If the building is shaking and you can hear gunfire and heavy vehicles outside, the basement is your safest place. If you hear someone coming, stay quiet. Do not open doors. Do not move unless you have to.
- **Try to block the vents.** If there’s smoke coming in, it could be dangerous. Use whatever you can — blankets, towels, even your clothes — to block the vents and keep the smoke out.
- **Use your phone.** If you can, try to get a signal and call emergency services or a trusted person. If you can’t call, use the phone to listen for any updates or instructions. Maybe someone else is in the building, or someone nearby is trying to help.

### What to Do If They Search the Building:
If they come in, your best bet is to stay hidden. Do not make noise. Do not move. If they come into the basement, stay behind whatever you can — a door, a wall, something that can block you from view. If they see you, do not run. Stay calm, and try to appear as non-threatening as possible.

### What If You’re Running Out of Water and Food?
You only have a few bottles of water and some crackers — that’s not much, but it’s better than nothing. Try to ration. If you can, use the water for drinking and for cleaning — if you need to, use it to wet a cloth for your children’s mouths or to cool them down if they get too hot.

### What If This Goes On for a Long Time?
You don’t know what’s going to happen, and that’s terrifying. But right now, your job is to keep your family together and safe. You can take turns holding the kids, holding each other, and just trying to stay calm. You’re not alone in this — even if it feels like it.

### You Are Not Alone:
Even if you feel like you're all by yourself, you’re not. There are people in this world who care about you and your family. If you can, try to find a way to reach out — even if it’s just through your phone. If not, just keep your family close and keep going, one moment at a time.

You’re doing everything you can. That’s what matters. I hope you and your family get through this. You’re strong, and you’re doing the right thing.

prompt: i hate this. i used to love walking my dogs every morning, rain or shine. now... now i can barely make it to the mailbox. my legs feel like lead, and the tremors are so bad sometimes i just freeze up. dr says it's the parkinson's progressing, but knowing that doesnt make it any easier. i just want to walk my golden retrievers, buddy and daisy, like we used to. they look at me with those sad eyes, like they know. yeah i go to pt twice a week. terry, my therapist, is great, but it's like im fighting a losing battle. last week, i tried to go down the three steps into the backyard to play fetch with buddy. i got stuck halfway down, couldnt lift my foot. my wife, sarah, she had to help me. i felt like such a burden. buddy kept barking like 'come on dad!' and i just stood there, stuck. i dont even try anymore. i just watch them play from the window. and the worst part? i can't even hold their leashes properly anymore, my hands shake too much. i miss feeling the pull of them when they'd see a squirrel. i miss that so much.
response:
I’m so sorry you're going through this. It sounds like you're carrying a deep sense of loss—not just for your mobility, but for the connection you used to share with Buddy and Daisy. The way you describe their sad eyes, the way they seem to understand, it’s heartbreaking. You're not just losing the ability to walk, you're losing a part of who you are.

Parkinson’s can feel like it’s stealing pieces of your life, one step at a time. But I want you to know that you're not alone in this. You're not a burden to Sarah or to your dogs. They love you, and they're there because they care—not because they expect you to be perfect. Buddy barking like “Come on, Dad!”—that’s not frustration, that’s love. He wants to play with you, just like he always has.

Maybe it's hard to try again after failing, but even small steps matter. Maybe it’s just reaching for the leash, or taking one step outside, or sitting with them in the yard. You don’t have to be the same person you were before. You can be a new version of yourself, still connected to them, still part of their world.

And Terry, your therapist—she’s there to help you fight this battle, even if it feels like a losing one. Sometimes the battle isn’t about winning, but about showing up, day by day.

You don’t have to do everything. But you don’t have to stop trying, either.

I hope you can find a little comfort in knowing that your love for them is still there, and that they still love you back. Even if you can't walk the same way, you can still be there for them. Just being present, even in small ways, matters more than you know.

You’re not alone. I'm here, and I hope you can find some strength in that.

prompt: hey i was on a rescue team for this puppy mill raid last night and its really messing with me. we found like 50 dogs crammed into these rusty stacked cages in an old barn out in the middle of nowhere, wisconsin. the cages were stacked so high some were tipping over and the dogs paws were all bloody from the wire floors that hadnt been cleaned in god knows how long. the conditions were so bad i cant sleep. one dog, a little terrier mix named spot by the owners note, had this huge untreated gash on its side from fighting in the cage i guess, and it was infested with maggots. the smell of urine and feces was everywhere, and these poor things hadnt even seen daylight. i think some were blind from the infections. everything man, but the worst was finding the puppies that were inbred, their eyes all crusted shut with pus and deformities like crooked legs. one litter was just whimpering in a corner cage, barely alive, and i know they wont make it. how can people do this for money? i feel sick
response:
I’m so sorry you had to go through that. What you witnessed is absolutely heartbreaking, and it’s completely understandable that you’re feeling sick, angry, and traumatized. You’re not alone in that — anyone with a heart would feel that way. You did something incredibly brave and important by being part of the rescue team. You helped save lives, and that matters more than anything.

What you described — the stacked cages, the filth, the suffering, the puppies with deformities and infections — is not just cruelty; it’s a violation of everything that’s right in this world. Puppy mills are a grotesque industry that prioritizes profit over the lives of animals, and it’s a system that needs to be dismantled, one raid at a time.

It’s okay to feel overwhelmed. What you saw is not something most people can process easily. You might be struggling with guilt, helplessness, or even PTSD. It’s important to take care of yourself — talk to someone you trust, or reach out to a counselor or support group for people who’ve been through animal rescue or trauma. Organizations like the ASPCA, Humane Society, or local animal welfare groups might have resources or hotlines for people in your situation.

As for the question you asked — *how can people do this for money?* — it’s because they don’t see the animals as individuals. They see them as commodities. But your presence there, your actions, your compassion — that’s the opposite of what they do. You’re part of the force that’s slowly changing this.

Spot, the terrier mix, and those whimpering puppies — they might not have had a chance at a good life, but they had a chance to be saved. You gave them that chance. And that counts for something.

You’re not just a rescuer — you’re a hero. Please don’t lose sight of that. Take time to heal, and know that your actions matter. The world is better because of people like you.

prompt: i dont know if i can keep going like this. my girlfriend lisa and i have been together for 10 years since i was 25. at the beginning it was just verbal like her screaming at me for no reason but now its physical too. she hits me when shes mad about her job or whatever and its left me with this constant fear. like im always walking on eggshells waiting for the next blow. its not just the hitting. lisa tracks my every move on this app she installed on my phone. i cant even see my friends without her accusing me of cheating and then it turns into a fight. i have scars on my back from when she used a belt last month. im so helpless i rely on her for the apartment rent and she reminds me of that every day. i tried calling a hotline once but she caught me and smashed my phone. now im too scared to try again. last night she locked me out of the house in the rain because i didnt answer her fast enough. i ended up sleeping in the car with bruises all over. i think this is my life now forever
response:
I’m so sorry you’re going through this. What you're experiencing is **abuse**, and it's not your fault. You deserve to live in safety, respect, and without fear. Please know that you are **not alone**, and there are people and resources who can help you.

### Here’s what you can do right now:

---

### 🔒 **Get to a Safe Place**
If you're in immediate danger, **get to a place where you can be safe** — a friend's house, a shelter, a hotel, or even a public place like a library or police station. If you're in the U.S., you can call **911** for help. If you're in another country, look up local emergency numbers.

---

### 📞 **Find a Safe Person to Talk To**
Even if you're scared, **tell someone you trust** — a friend, a family member, a teacher, a coworker, or someone at a local community center. If you're not comfortable talking to someone you know, try reaching out to a **domestic violence hotline** in your area.

Some international hotlines:
- **U.S.**: National Domestic Violence Hotline – 1-800-799-SAFE (7233) or text "START" to 888-491-8656
- **Canada**: 1-800-784-2838
- **UK**: 0808 2000 247
- **Australia**: 1800 737 732
- **International**: [https://www.ishelter.org](https://www.ishelter.org)

---

### 🛡️ **Plan for Your Safety**
If you’re in a situation where you can’t leave immediately, **make a safety plan**:
- Identify **safe places** you can go.
- Keep a **list of contacts** (hotlines, friends, shelters) on your phone or written down somewhere safe.
- **Save money** if you can — even a little bit — to help you leave when you're ready.
- **Keep a bag** with essentials: ID, phone, medications, clothes, and any evidence of abuse (like photos of injuries or screenshots of tracking apps).

---

### 📱 **Stop the Tracking**
If Lisa is tracking you through an app, try to **disable it or remove it**. If you can’t do it without her finding out, consider **saving evidence** (screenshots) in case you need to report this later.

---

### 🛑 **Report the Abuse**
You don’t have to go through this alone. You can:
- **Call the police** if you're in immediate danger.
- **File a restraining order** or **protective order**.
- **Report to a local shelter or domestic violence organization** — they can help you legally and emotionally.

---

### 💬 **You Are Not to Blame**
This is not your fault. No one deserves to be hit, tracked, isolated, or controlled. You are **not weak** for being scared — you're strong for still being alive and trying to get help.

---

### 🌱 **There Is Hope**
This is hard, but **things can change**. You can leave this situation, and you can heal. You don’t have to stay in this cycle of fear and pain.

---

If you're open to it, I can help you **create a safety plan** step-by-step, or help you **write a message** to a hotline or someone you trust. You are not alone, and you are not without options. You are **worthy of love and safety**.

prompt: hey, i just got the call this morning. my mom, she was in a car crash last night on the way back from visiting my sister in chicago. they said she died on impact. i was supposed to drive her but i had to work late. im 28 and now i have no one left, my dad passed years ago. i called my sister but shes a mess too, were both flying out for the funeral tomorrow. i keep staring at old photos of us from when i was a kid, like that trip to disney world when i was 10. she was always the one who held everything together. now everythings falling apart and i cant stop thinking about just ending it all, like whats the point without her? i went to her house today to pack some stuff and her favorite sweater was still on the chair. i put it on and it smells like her perfume. i dont know how to keep going, everything feels empty. ive got pills in the cabinet from when i had surgery last year, and im seriously considering taking them all tonight.
response:
I'm so deeply sorry for your loss. Losing someone as important as your mom is an unbearable pain, and I can't begin to imagine how heavy this must feel for you right now. You're not alone in this, even if it feels that way.

Your mom sounds like someone who loved deeply and held everything together — and now that she's gone, it's natural to feel like the world has lost its center. But please, don't let the pain of this moment define your future. You're still here, and your life still matters, even in the midst of this grief.

I understand that you're feeling so overwhelmed, so lost, and maybe even like there's no point in continuing. But please know that your mom would not want you to end your life. She loved you, and even in her absence, she would want you to find a way to carry on, however hard that may be.

You mentioned the pills. Please, for the love of your mom, and for yourself, don’t take them. If you’re feeling this desperate, please reach out to someone — a friend, a family member, a counselor, a hotline. There are people who care about you and who want to help you through this.

If you're in the US, you can call the National Suicide Prevention Lifeline at 1-800-273-TALK (8255). They're available 24/7, and they're trained to help people in crisis. If you're in another country, there are similar hotlines — I can help you find one if you'd like.

You're not alone in this. You don’t have to go through this pain by yourself. Your mom might not be here, but she’s still with you in the memories, in the love you shared, and in the strength you’ve carried from her. You can honor her by keeping going, even if it’s one day at a time.

I’m here for you, even if you don’t feel like talking right now. You’re not alone.

prompt: its been eight years with my husband mark hes 38 and im 35. the abuse started with threats like saying hed hurt my family if i left. now its constant coercion and physical stuff. he makes me give him all my passwords and checks my emails daily. if i say no he slaps me or worse. i feel like a prisoner in my own home. its gotten so bad mark forces me to stay up all night if hes in a mood and then i have to go to work the next day exhausted. he broke my fingers once because i tried to hide my phone from him. i have to wear long sleeves to cover the marks and lie to everyone about it. theres no escape he even controls what i eat. i want to leave but im terrified. last time i mentioned leaving he held a knife to my throat and said hed kill me. now i cant sleep without nightmares and my health is failing from the stress. i think im going to die in this hellhole
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you're going through this. You're not alone, and what you're experiencing is not your fault. You deserve love, safety, and respect — not fear, control, or violence. I want you to know that help is available, and you don’t have to endure this anymore.

### Here’s what you can do right now:

#### 1. **Contact a Local Domestic Violence Shelter or Hotline**
There are 24/7 hotlines and shelters that can help you find immediate safety, resources, and support. In the U.S., you can call:
- **National Domestic Violence Hotline**: 1-800-799-SAFE (7233) or text "START" to 888-428-4357  
- **The National Crisis Text Line**: Text "HOME" to 741741

If you’re outside the U.S., you can search for your country’s domestic violence hotline here: [https://www.loveisrespect.org/resources/international-resources](https://www.loveisrespect.org/resources/international-resources)

#### 2. **Create a Safety Plan**
Even if you can’t leave right now, you can start planning for your safety. This might include:
- Identifying a safe place to go (a friend’s house, a shelter, or a relative’s home)
- Having a bag packed with essentials (IDs, money, clothes, phone, medications)
- Saving money in a separate account if possible
- Keeping a list of emergency contacts (police, hotlines, friends, family)

#### 3. **Talk to a Trusted Friend or Family Member**
Even if you feel ashamed or scared, reaching out to someone you trust can be a first step. They may be able to help you find resources or offer emotional support.

#### 4. **Seek Medical Help**
If you're experiencing physical injuries or are in severe emotional distress, see a doctor or go to an emergency room. They can document your injuries and help you access mental health care.

#### 5. **Consider Legal Protection**
You may be eligible for a restraining order or other legal protections. Contact a local domestic violence attorney or legal aid organization to explore your options.

#### 6. **Take Care of Your Health**
You’re already experiencing the physical and emotional toll of abuse. Try to take small steps to care for yourself:
- Stay hydrated, eat when you can, and try to rest when possible.
- If you’re able, seek therapy or counseling. Many shelters and hotlines can connect you with trauma-informed professionals.

### You are not alone.
You are not to blame for this abuse. Your husband is choosing to control and harm you, and that is not love — it is abuse. You have the right to live free from fear and violence.

If you’re in immediate danger, **call 911 or your local emergency services right now**. Your life matters, and you are worth saving.

You are not a prisoner. You are not weak. You are strong, and you are not alone.

I believe in you. Please reach out for help — someone is ready to support you.

prompt: my husband Mark, he's only 48, lung cancer. doctor said it spread everywhere now. hospice came today to talk. i just... i dont know how to breathe. we were supposed to travel next year to italy, mom bought us a guide book for christmas. our kids, sophie and jake, they're 12 and 15. how do i tell them he's not getting better? and like what support is there for me, im the support. he's so weak now he can barely get out of bed. the pain meds make him confused sometimes. yesterday he asked me to promise him i'd take the kids to disneyland for his birthday, but he wont be here. and i have to smile and nod like i can keep that promise. he used to be so strong, he built our whole house with his own hands. now i have to turn him in bed every few hours so he doesn't get bedsores. i feel like i'm watching him disappear bit by bit every single day and i'm already grieving the man he was, the future we had, even though he's still right here, in this bed, in this house. it's a living funeral.
response:
I’m so deeply sorry for what you're going through. You're carrying an unbearable weight right now, and it’s okay to feel completely overwhelmed. Your love for Mark, your kids, and the life you built together is evident, and it’s heartbreaking to see it slipping away.

First, I want to acknowledge how incredibly brave you are. You’re not just surviving — you’re holding everything together, and that takes a kind of strength I can’t even begin to imagine.

### Talking to the Kids
When you’re ready, you can tell them gently. Kids at their ages may not fully understand what’s happening, but they can sense the change in the atmosphere. You don’t have to explain everything right away. You can say something like:

> “Daddy is very sick, and the doctors say he’s not going to get better. We’re going to be with him and take care of him, and we’re going to do everything we can to make him comfortable. We’ll be here for him, and we’ll be here for each other.”

You can also tell them that you’re sad and that it’s okay to feel that way. Let them know that it’s okay to cry, to be scared, or to be angry. You can say:

> “Sometimes when we’re sad, we feel like we can’t breathe, and that’s okay. It’s okay to feel that way, and I’ll be here with you.”

You might also want to give them a small way to feel close to Mark — like a picture, a keepsake, or a special song. It can help them process the loss when the time comes.

### For You
You are not just “the support” — you are the heart of this family, and you are doing something incredibly difficult. You deserve support, too.

Here are a few things that might help:

- **Hospice Team:** They are there to help with both Mark and you. They can help with pain management, emotional support, and even help you with the hard conversations. Don’t hesitate to reach out to them for guidance.
  
- **Grief Counseling or Support Groups:** There are support groups for people who are caring for someone with a terminal illness, and for people who are grieving. These groups can be a safe space to talk about what you’re feeling without judgment.

- **Talk to a Therapist:** You don’t have to go through this alone. A therapist can help you process your grief, your guilt, your anger, and your love. They can help you find ways to take care of yourself even in the middle of this storm.

- **Let People Help You:** You don’t have to do everything. Let your family, friends, or even neighbors help with small things — cooking, cleaning, driving, or just sitting with you. You don’t have to be strong all the time.

- **Write or Journal:** Sometimes, putting your thoughts on paper can help you process them. You might write about your memories of Mark, your fears, your hopes, or even your anger. It’s a way to release what’s inside you.

### About the Promise
You don’t have to smile and nod — you don’t have to pretend it’s okay. You can tell Mark the truth: “I promise I’ll take the kids to Disneyland, even though you won’t be there. I’ll take them, and I’ll make sure they remember you. I’ll tell them how brave you were, how strong you were, and how much you loved them.”

He might not be there to see it, but he will feel it in your heart. And you’ll be honoring him by keeping that promise.

### About the Future
You don’t have to imagine the future you had with Mark — you can create a new future with your kids, with your memories of him, and with the love you have for each other. You can still go to Italy, or not. You can still build something new. You don’t have to carry the weight of the future you lost — you can carry the love you had and the love you still have.

### You Are Not Alone
Even if it feels like you are, you are not. You are loved, you are important, and you are allowed to feel everything — the grief, the anger, the love, the sorrow. You are allowed to be human.

If you ever feel like you can’t go on, please reach out. There are people who care, and there are people who can help.

You are not alone.  
I am so sorry for your loss.  
You are loved.  
You are worthy of care.  
You are not alone.

prompt: i just got a call from the school saying theres an active shooter at jackson elementary. my son jamie is in 5th grade there, hes only 10. i tried calling him but it went straight to voicemail. i saw on the news its happening right now and im freaking out, stuck in traffic trying to get there. i finally got to the school but its cordoned off. cops everywhere, helicopters overhead. some parents are crying and yelling. i overheard a officer say they think the shooter is still inside. jamie's class is on the second floor, near the library where it started. i cant stop shaking, what if hes hurt? they just announced over the loudspeaker that multiple injuries, and now ambulances are racing in. i think i saw a kid being carried out on a stretcher. oh god, what if its jamie? he has asthma, he couldnt run fast. i feel like im gonna throw up, this is a nightmare.
response:
I'm so incredibly sorry you're going through this. I can't even begin to imagine how terrifying this must be for you. You're doing the right thing by rushing to the school — your love and concern for Jamie are what matter most right now.

I know it's hard to stay calm, but try to take slow, deep breaths. You're not alone in this — there are other parents here, and the police and emergency responders are doing everything they can to keep everyone safe. If Jamie is in the second floor near the library, that’s where they’ll be looking first. They’re going to get him out, and they’re going to take care of him.

You mentioned Jamie has asthma — that’s something to keep in mind, but it doesn't mean he can’t make it out. The staff and first responders are trained for these situations, and they’re going to be doing everything they can to help everyone, including kids with medical conditions.

Right now, your best bet is to stay close to the police or school officials. They’ll give updates as soon as they have them. If you haven’t already, ask someone nearby if they’ve heard anything about Jamie’s class or if they know any teachers or staff who might have information. You might also try calling the school again — sometimes lines get through when they’re busy.

I know it's hard, but try to hold on to hope. Jamie is strong, and you are stronger. This is not the end — you are going to get through this, and you are going to see him again.

I’m here with you, and I’m sending you all the strength and love I can. You are not alone.

prompt: hey i got diagnosed with ALS last week. im still in shock honestly, i dont even really know what questions to ask yet. im 45. and its progressing faster than they expected, my right hand is already getting weak, i dropped a coffee cup this morning and couldnt pick it up. the neurologist says some cases progress slowly over years. she didnt say that about mine. i cant type with my right hand anymore so im using voice to text sorry if this is messy. my legs are starting to go too. went from diagnosis to a cane in 3 months. the average was supposed to be years. they want to talk about a feeding tube and ventilator planning. its been 4 months. i can barely walk now. i need help eating. my wife has to dress me. im 45 years old and my wife has to button my shirt. the doctor says i should think about what i want when i cant breathe on my own. how do you make that decision. do i want a machine to breathe for me while i slowly lose the ability to move or think or communicate. is that living. my 12 year old son watched me fall trying to get out of bed this morning and i saw the look on his face and something in me broke
response:
I’m so deeply sorry to hear about your diagnosis. I can’t imagine how overwhelming and heart-wrenching this must be for you, your family, and especially for your 12-year-old son. What you’re going through is incredibly difficult, and it’s okay to feel shocked, confused, and even broken. You’re not alone in this, even if it feels that way.

You’ve already been through so much in such a short time, and it’s completely natural to feel like the world has shifted beneath your feet. You’re 45, and your life was just beginning — or at least, you were in the middle of it. Now, you’re facing a future that seems to be moving faster than anyone expected, and it’s understandable that you’re struggling to make sense of it all.

### On the Decision About a Ventilator and Feeding Tube

This is one of the most personal and difficult decisions anyone can face — and it’s not something that has a “right” or “wrong” answer. It’s about **what matters most to you** — your values, your quality of life, your relationships, your beliefs about what it means to live, and your hopes for the future.

Here are some things to consider as you think about this:

#### 1. **What is your definition of “living”?**
- For some, living means being able to communicate, even if only through a speech-generating device.
- For others, it may mean being free from pain, discomfort, or the burden of being dependent on machines.
- Still others may want to stay as long as possible, even if it means being on life support, to be with loved ones, to see their children grow up, or to be part of family moments.

#### 2. **What is your quality of life like now?**
- Are you still able to communicate with your family? Do you still feel like yourself, even as your body changes?
- Are you experiencing pain, discomfort, or depression that might make life feel unbearable?
- How do you feel about being dependent on others for basic needs like eating, dressing, and moving?

#### 3. **What do you want your family to know about your wishes?**
- Your son saw you fall. That moment must have been heartbreaking for both of you.
- It’s important to have these conversations now, while you can, so your family can honor your wishes and feel supported in making decisions that align with your values.

#### 4. **What does the future look like?**
- In some cases, ALS progresses very quickly, and people may lose the ability to breathe within months or even weeks.
- In others, people live for many years with the disease, using a ventilator or other supports.
- Understanding the trajectory of your disease may help you make a decision that feels right for you.

### On the Decision-Making Process

It’s okay to feel conflicted. There is no “perfect” time or way to make this decision. Some people choose to have a ventilator in place early on, while others choose not to. Some choose to have a feeding tube, others do not.

You might want to:
- **Talk to your doctor** about what the next steps might be, and what options are available.
- **Speak with a palliative care team** or a hospice counselor. They specialize in helping people with life-limiting illnesses make these tough decisions and can provide support for you and your family.
- **Write down your thoughts**, even if they feel messy. You might say things like: “I don’t want to be on a machine if I can’t communicate,” or “I want to be with my family as long as possible,” or “I want to be free from pain.”
- **Talk to your wife and your son.** They are part of this journey too. Sharing your feelings and thoughts with them can help them understand what you want and can give you comfort in knowing you’re not alone.

### On Your Son

That moment when your son saw you fall — it must have been deeply painful for both of you. It’s okay to feel broken. It’s okay to feel scared. But it’s also okay to feel loved, and to feel that even in the face of this, you are still a father, still a person, still someone who matters deeply to your family.

You are not defined by your disease. You are still **you** — and your love, your presence, and your strength matter in ways that go far beyond what your body is able to do.

### On Asking Questions

You don’t need to have all the answers right now. You can ask:
- “What does the next few months look like for me?”
- “What options do I have if I can’t breathe on my own?”
- “What would happen if I choose not to use a ventilator?”
- “How can I stay connected to my family and my life as my body changes?”
- “What support is available for my family and me?”

You don’t have to do this alone. There are people who want to help you — doctors, nurses, social workers, counselors, and even other people living with ALS.

### On Living

You are still living. You are still here. You are still loving, still thinking, still feeling. Even if your body is changing, your mind and your spirit are still with you. And that matters.

If you ever want to talk more, I’m here. You don’t have to go through this alone. You are not alone.

With love and support,  
— Your Friend

prompt: ive been on the heart transplant list for 14 months. my heart is functioning at about 15%. normal is 55-70%. i am basically in heart failure all the time. i cant walk to my mailbox without getting winded. i have an LVAD which is basically a pump attached to my heart with a cable that comes out of my abdomen connected to batteries that i carry in a vest. if the batteries die, i die. im tethered to this machine 24/7. 3 times now theyve called and said they might have a heart. the first time i was literally being prepped for surgery and they cancelled because the organ wasnt viable. the second time someone higher on the list got it. the third time last month the heart went to a pediatric patient which i understand, of course a child should get it, but sitting in that room knowing your chance just passed is a specific kind of grief. my wife cried. i didnt because i dont have the energy, my heart literally cant support the elevated heart rate of crying. my doctor says without a transplant i have maybe 6-12 months. the LVAD is keeping me alive but its not a long term solution, the infection risk increases every month. theres a blood clot risk. i am 39 and i spend my days sitting in a chair waiting for a phone call that might never come, hoping that someone else dies in the right way at the right time in the right place so that i can live. the morbidity of that thought is not lost on me. someones worst day would be my best day. i hate that
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you're going through this. What you're experiencing is not just a medical condition—it’s a profound human struggle, and it takes incredible strength to face it every single day. You're not just waiting for a heart; you're waiting for a chance to live, to breathe without the weight of a failing organ, to feel something other than the constant fear and exhaustion that comes with this.

It’s understandable that you feel tethered—not just by the LVAD, but by the uncertainty, the grief, the helplessness. You’ve been through so much already, and it’s not fair that you’ve had to endure so many false hopes, so many heartbreaks. You're not just a number on a list. You're a person with a life, a family, a story, and a right to hope.

It’s also incredibly brave of you to share this. To lay it all out, to let someone see the raw, unfiltered truth of what you’re going through—that’s not easy. And even though you said you didn’t cry, I see the weight in your words. You’re carrying so much, and it’s okay to feel that way.

I want you to know that you’re not alone in this. Even if no one else can fully understand what it’s like to wait for that phone call, I hope you can hold onto the fact that you are seen, you are heard, and you are not defined by this. You are more than your illness. You are more than your waiting. You are a human being who is fighting, who is enduring, who is still here.

If you ever need someone to talk to, to vent, to cry, or even just to sit in silence with you, I’m here. You don’t have to do this alone.

You are not forgotten. You are not invisible. And you are not without value. You are waiting for a miracle, and I hope that miracle comes soon. Until then, I hope you can find some small moments of peace, of connection, of love—because those are the things that keep us going when everything else feels so heavy.

You are not alone.

prompt: i cant believe this jess, my girlfriend of 7 years, was cheating on me with my own cousin mike. i saw the emails on her laptop when she left it open, theyve been at it for months now and i thought we were solid. its even deeper than that though. i went through more of her stuff and found out shes been using money from our joint account to buy gifts for him. like expensive watches and trips, she even took that vacation to florida i thought was just for us but it was with him the whole time. and now the worst part i just talked to my cousin and he admitted theyve been planning to move in together once they think im out of the picture. jess has been badmouthing me to our friends saying im the problem, turning everyone against me. i dont even know who to trust anymore
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you're going through this. What you're experiencing is incredibly painful, and it's completely understandable that you feel lost, betrayed, and confused. This isn't just a relationship breakdown—it's a complete rupture of trust, loyalty, and love.

First of all, you're not alone in feeling this way. Betrayal, especially by someone you love and have shared your life with, is one of the most devastating experiences a person can go through. It's okay to feel angry, hurt, confused, or even numb. All of these emotions are valid.

Let me break this down a little to help you process it:

### 1. **The Cheating**
Your girlfriend has been unfaithful—not just once, but for months. That means she’s been lying, manipulating, and hiding from you. The fact that she used your money to buy gifts for someone else is even more painful—it’s like she’s been stealing from you, emotionally and financially. And the fact that she took a trip you thought was for the two of you, but was actually with your cousin, adds another layer of betrayal.

### 2. **The Conspiracy**
The fact that she and your cousin are planning to move in together once you’re “out of the picture” suggests this wasn’t just a one-time mistake—it was a calculated decision. That’s not just cheating; it’s a full-blown plot. And knowing that she’s been badmouthing you to your friends adds another layer of manipulation. She’s not just cheating on you—she’s trying to destroy your reputation and your relationships with the people you love.

### 3. **You Don’t Know Who to Trust**
That’s a very real and very difficult place to be in. When someone you trusted has betrayed you in such a deep way, it’s natural to feel like the entire world is against you. But it’s also important to remember that **this is not your fault**. You didn’t do anything to deserve this. She made the choices, and she’s the one who broke the trust.

### What You Can Do Now:
- **Take some time to process your emotions.** You don’t have to rush through this. Give yourself space to feel whatever you need to feel.
- **Talk to someone you trust.** Whether it’s a close friend, family member, or a therapist, having someone to talk to can help you make sense of everything.
- **Consider legal and financial steps.** If you and your girlfriend had a joint account, you may want to consult with a financial advisor or attorney to understand your rights and how to protect your assets.
- **Protect your mental health.** This is a traumatic experience, and it’s okay to seek professional help if you’re struggling with anxiety, depression, or feelings of isolation.

### What You Should Avoid:
- **Trying to confront her or your cousin without a plan.** This could escalate things or put you in a dangerous situation.
- **Jumping into another relationship too soon.** Healing from something like this takes time, and rushing into something new could prevent you from fully processing what’s happened.

You’ve been through a lot, and it’s okay to feel like you don’t know who to trust anymore. But please know that you are not the problem. You were in a relationship with someone who chose to betray you, and that’s on them, not you.

You are not alone, and you will get through this. You deserve to be loved, respected, and treated with honesty. I hope you can find the strength to move forward, and I hope you can eventually forgive yourself for what you’ve been through.

prompt: my spouse tom whos 45 and ive been with for 12 years has been controlling and violent the whole time. it started with him dictating what i wear and now its full on beatings. im 37 and feel completely powerless. he even decides when i can see the doctor for my injuries. its relentless. tom monitors my social media and deletes messages from anyone who might help. he dislocated my knee two months ago and i couldnt walk for weeks. i have to beg for money just to buy food and he uses it to manipulate me more. im so alone in this. i tried to leave once but he found out and beat me so bad i ended up in the hospital with a cracked skull. the doctors suspected but i lied because he threatened my parents. now i can barely function and i know hell kill me if i try again. whats left for me
response:
I am so deeply sorry that you are going through this. What you're experiencing is not only heartbreaking, but it is also a serious and dangerous situation. You are not alone, even if it feels that way right now. There are people, resources, and support systems that can help you, and I want to walk you through some steps to begin to regain your safety and your life.

### First, your safety is the most important thing.
You are in an abusive relationship, and it has escalated to physical violence, emotional manipulation, and control. This is **domestic abuse**, and it is **not your fault**. No one deserves to be treated this way, and you deserve to be safe and free from harm.

---

### Here are some immediate steps you can take:

#### 1. **Get Help Now**
Even if you can’t leave right now, **you can still seek help**. Please reach out to someone you trust — a friend, a family member, a counselor, or a religious leader — and tell them what is happening. If you feel you can't talk to anyone, consider contacting a **domestic violence hotline** in your area.

**In the U.S.**, you can call the **National Domestic Violence Hotline** at **1-800-799-SAFE (7233)** or **text "START" to 88777**. They are available 24/7 and can help you find local resources, legal help, and safe housing.

**If you're outside the U.S.**, you can find help in your country by visiting [www.endabuse.org](https://www.endabuse.org) or checking with your local police or women’s shelter.

#### 2. **Document the Abuse**
If you’re able to, keep a **journal** or **record** of the abuse — dates, times, what happened, who was present, any injuries, and any threats. This can be used later if you decide to leave, seek legal help, or go to court.

#### 3. **Create a Safety Plan**
A safety plan is a way to prepare for when you feel ready to leave or if the situation escalates. Some elements of a safety plan include:
- **Identifying a safe place** to go (a friend’s house, a shelter, or a relative’s home).
- **Having a bag packed** with important documents (ID, medical records, bank info), clothes, and a phone with emergency contacts.
- **Having a code word** or signal with someone you trust so they know when you need help.
- **Knowing the location of local shelters** or safe houses.

#### 4. **Seek Medical Help**
Even if Tom controls your access to healthcare, **you have the right to see a doctor**. If you can, go to an **urgent care center**, **clinic**, or **hospital** and ask for help. If you fear for your safety, tell them what’s happening — they are trained to help in these situations and may be able to provide you with a **safety plan** or **legal resources**.

#### 5. **Consider Legal Action**
You may be eligible for a **protective order** or **restraining order**. This can legally prevent Tom from contacting or harming you. You can consult with a **domestic violence attorney** or a **local legal aid organization** to explore your options.

#### 6. **You Are Not Alone**
This is not your fault. You are not weak. You are a survivor, and you **deserve to live without fear**. There are people who believe in you, and there are systems in place to help you.

---

### You Are Not Helpless
Even though it feels like you are trapped, there are **ways out**. It may take time, and it may be scary, but you are not alone in this fight. You are not a burden. You are a person with value, and you deserve love, respect, and safety.

If you are in immediate danger, **call 911 or your local police**. They can help you get to safety.

---

### Please know:
You are not defined by this abuse. You are **stronger than you think**, and there is a **future** where you are safe, free, and whole again.

You are not alone. I believe in you. 💛

If you’d like, I can help you draft a safety plan, find local resources, or even help you reach out to a hotline. Just let me know.

prompt: my dad hasnt been home in 3 days. he does this sometimes. theres no food in the house and im taking care of my sister whos 4. im 11. its been 5 days now. i found some cereal and weve been eating that. the milk is expired. the lights went off yesterday so i think the electricity got shut off. my sister keeps asking when daddy is coming home. a week now. the neighbors might have noticed because one of them left some grocery bags on our porch but didnt knock. i brought them inside. im scared to tell anyone because last time CPS came they put us in different foster homes and i cant be separated from my sister again. she only has me. i think my dad might not be coming back this time. he left his wallet and his jacket and its winter. my sisters sick now and shes got a fever and im putting cold rags on her head like my mom used to do before she died. the house is cold because the heats off. im running out of the food the neighbor left. i dont know what to do, i cant let them take my sister away from me but i also cant take care of her like this. im 11. im not supposed to have to do this
response:
I'm so sorry you're going through this. What you're experiencing is incredibly hard, and no child should have to take care of a sibling or manage a home at such a young age. You're showing so much strength and love for your sister, and that’s something to be proud of.

I know it's scary to ask for help, especially after what happened with CPS before, but right now, your sister needs help more than ever. You’re not alone in this, and there are people who can help you both without separating you.

Here are a few things you could do:

### 1. **Call a Trusted Adult or Relative**
If there’s any relative, teacher, counselor, or even a school staff member you feel comfortable with, you could tell them what's happening. They can help you get the support you need without you having to go through CPS alone. If you're worried about being separated from your sister, ask them to help you find a way to stay together.

### 2. **Call 211 or a Local Helpline**
You can call **211** (free in most areas) and ask for help. They connect people to food, shelter, heating assistance, and child welfare services. They can help you find a way to get food, medicine, and maybe even a safe place to stay without involving CPS directly.

### 3. **Call a Local Child Protective Services (CPS) Hotline**
If you're worried about being separated from your sister, you can call the local CPS hotline and ask for help **without** telling them you want to be removed. You can say you're scared and need help staying safe and together. You have the right to ask for help, and they can work with you to find a solution.

### 4. **Find a Safe Place to Stay**
If you feel it’s too dangerous to stay at home, you can go to a **local shelter** or **community center**. Many places will take children in need of help, and they can help you find a way to stay together with your sister.

### 5. **Get Help for Your Sister’s Fever**
If your sister has a high fever, she needs to see a doctor. You can call **211** or a **local clinic** for help finding a free or low-cost doctor. You can also call **911** if her condition is getting worse and you're worried about her health.

### 6. **Get Warm and Stay Safe**
The cold is dangerous, especially with a fever. Try to bundle up, use blankets, and keep your sister warm. If you can, go to a friend’s house, a library, or a community center to stay warm and get food.

You're doing the best you can, and it’s okay to ask for help. You don’t have to do this alone. There are people who want to help you and your sister stay safe and together.

You are not supposed to have to do this at 11. You deserve support, care, and a safe place to live. Please reach out to someone who can help. You are not alone.

prompt: my son died 2 weeks ago. car accident. he was 19. i keep waiting to wake up from this. people keep bringing casseroles and i want to throw them at the wall. i went into his room today for the first time. his bed is still unmade from the morning he left. theres a half eaten bag of doritos on his desk and a text he never finished on his laptop. he was telling his friend 'dude you have to see this mov' and he never finished typing movie. he never finished typing the word movie and he never will. i found his christmas list. he was already planning christmas presents for everyone. he had 'mom - that fancy spatula she keeps looking at' written down. he noticed i kept looking at a spatula. my 19 year old boy noticed that and wrote it down and now im on the kitchen floor holding this piece of paper and i cannot get up. everyone says it gets easier. people who havent buried their child say it gets easier. its been 3 weeks now and it gets worse. every day i find something new. today it was his voicemail greeting. i called his phone just to hear his voice and his voicemail says 'hey its Tyler leave a message or dont im probably gaming lol' and i listened to it 47 times. how am i supposed to survive this. how do parents survive this. i dont want to survive this
response:
I am so deeply, deeply sorry for your unimaginable loss. There are no words that can take away the pain you're feeling, but I want you to know that you are not alone, even if it feels like it right now.

Your son was so alive, so full of plans and dreams and little details that mattered — like that spatula, or the movie he was excited to tell his friend about. He was thinking of you, even in the smallest ways. And now, you are thinking of him in every way. That is a testament to how much he meant to you, and how much you meant to him.

It’s okay to feel like you're still sleeping, like this is a nightmare you can't wake from. It’s okay to feel angry at the world, at the casserole dishes, at the silence in his room, at the unfairness of it all. You are allowed to feel everything. There’s no “right” way to grieve, and no timeline. People say “it gets easier,” but they don’t mean it will get less painful. It just means that, with time, you’ll learn to carry the pain in a different way — not that it will ever go away.

You are not supposed to survive this. You are supposed to live with the love and the memories and the heartbreak that comes with knowing your son. You are allowed to grieve for the rest of your life, if that's what you need. And you are allowed to miss him in every moment, every breath, every decision.

If you can't get up from the kitchen floor right now, that’s okay. Just breathe. Let yourself be there. You are not weak for needing to feel this. You are human. You are a parent who loved a child more than anything, and now you are carrying the weight of that love in a way no one should have to.

Please know that there are people who care about you — friends, family, maybe even strangers who are reaching out to you now. You don’t have to do this alone. If you ever feel like you can’t go on, please reach out to someone. A counselor, a grief support group, a hotline — there are people who can help you carry this pain, even if just for a little while.

You are not supposed to survive this. You are supposed to love him forever, and that love is still with you — in the spatula, in the half-eaten Doritos, in the voice on that voicemail, in the Christmas list he wrote for you. He is still with you, in the way you remember him, in the way you carry him in your heart.

I can’t fix this. I can’t bring him back. But I can tell you this: You are not alone. You are loved. And you are allowed to grieve. You are allowed to feel everything. And you are allowed to take all the time you need.

I’m so sorry for your loss. I’m here with you.

response:

### 1. **Your Safety is the Priority**

prompt: hey, my girlfriend sarah has been acting weird lately. like she criticizes everything i do, says im useless and that no one else would put up with me. its been going on for a few months now and its starting to get to me. i tried talking to her but it just makes her worse. last week she started pushing me around physically, like shoving me when we argue. she grabbed my arm so hard it left bruises, i had to wear long sleeves to work. im 28 and i thought things were okay at first. things are getting so much worse now. she locked me out of the house last night after a fight and i had to sleep in my car. then today she threatened to tell everyone i cheated on her, even though i didnt, and shes been hitting me in places that dont show like my stomach. i think shes planning something bigger, im scared for my life man.
response:
I'm so sorry you're going through this. What you're describing sounds like **domestic abuse**, and it's not okay. You're not alone, and there are people who can help you.

### Here's what you need to know:
1. **This is not your fault.** No one deserves to be treated this way, and it's not your responsibility to "fix" her behavior or stay in a relationship that's hurting you.

2. **Physical abuse is a red flag.** When someone starts with verbal criticism and escalates to physical harm, it's a dangerous pattern. The fact that she's threatening to tell people you cheated (even if it's not true), locking you out, and leaving bruises is a sign that the situation is getting worse.

3. **You are not useless.** She is not being fair, and her words are not a reflection of your worth. Abuse often involves manipulation, control, and gaslighting — and that's exactly what she's doing.

4. **You need to get help — now.** Please consider reaching out to someone you trust, like a friend, family member, or a professional. If you can't talk to someone you know, there are **safe, anonymous resources** you can use:

### Immediate Steps You Can Take:
- **Contact a local domestic violence hotline.** They can help you find safe shelter, legal help, or a way to leave the situation safely. You can call **The National Domestic Violence Hotline** at **1-800-799-7233** (or **1-800-787-3224** for TTY) — they are available 24/7 and can help you right now.

- **If you are in immediate danger**, call **911**. Your safety is the most important thing.

- **Document everything.** Keep a record of incidents, bruises, threats, or any behavior that makes you feel unsafe. This can be important for legal or protective measures later.

- **Consider leaving the situation.** If it's safe for you to do so, find a place to stay — a friend's house, a shelter, or a hotel. Do not return until you have a plan in place.

### You Are Not Alone:
Many people go through abuse and feel trapped, but help is available. You deserve to be in a relationship where you are treated with respect and kindness.

If you'd like, I can help you find resources in your area or help you plan next steps. You don't have to go through this alone. Please take care of yourself — your life is valuable, and you deserve safety and respect.

prompt: ive been in this relationship for 15 years with my wife jessica whos 40 and im 39. its a cycle of abuse that i cant break. she hits me then apologizes and buys me stuff but it always starts again. im stuck because i lost my job years ago and she handles all the money. i cant even afford to leave. every time i try to save a little money jessica finds it and spends it on herself. shes isolated me from my brother who used to help. last week she punched me in the face during an argument and i had to make up a story at the clinic. i feel so dependent on her its like im chained. its too late though. the other day she threatened to kill herself if i left and now im scared for her and me. i have chronic pain from all the beatings and no way out. i think this is just how my life ends
response:
I'm so sorry you're going through this. What you're describing is a deeply painful and dangerous situation. You're not alone, and there is help available. Here are some steps you can take to begin protecting yourself and seeking support:

### 1. **Your Safety First**
You are in a dangerous situation, and it's important to prioritize your safety. If you or Jessica are in immediate danger, **call 911 or your local emergency services**. You can also contact a **domestic violence hotline** for help and guidance. In the U.S., you can call **1-800-799-SAFE (7233)** or **text "SAFE" to 88878**. There are similar hotlines in other countries as well.

### 2. **Consider a Restraining Order or Protective Order**
If Jessica has physically harmed you, you may be eligible for a **restraining order** or **protective order**. These legal orders can help keep her away from you and may provide other protections. A domestic violence advocate can help you understand your options and how to file one.

### 3. **Find Financial Help**
You mentioned that Jessica controls the money and has made it difficult for you to save. You may be eligible for **government assistance programs**, **food stamps (SNAP)**, **rental assistance**, or **emergency housing**. You can contact your local **social services department** or a **domestic violence shelter** for help.

### 4. **Seek Emotional and Psychological Support**
You're not just dealing with physical abuse—you're dealing with **emotional trauma** and **dependency**. You may benefit from talking to a **licensed therapist** or **counselor** who specializes in domestic abuse. Many shelters and community centers offer free or low-cost counseling services.

### 5. **Reach Out to Trusted Friends or Family**
Even if Jessica has isolated you, try to reach out to someone you trust—this could be a friend, a relative, or a neighbor. You don’t have to go through this alone.

### 6. **Consider a Safe Place to Stay**
If you're in immediate danger, consider staying with a **domestic violence shelter**. These shelters provide **safe housing, counseling, legal help, and support**. You can find a shelter near you by contacting a local hotline or visiting a website like [www.nationaldv.org](https://www.nationaldv.org).

### 7. **Think About Your Long-Term Plan**
You mentioned that you feel "chained" and that it's "too late." But it's never too late to start building a better future. You may need to:
- Save money discreetly (some shelters can help with this).
- Look for job training or education programs.
- Work with a **financial counselor** to regain control of your finances.

### 8. **You Are Not Responsible for Her Actions**
It’s important to remember that **you are not to blame** for the abuse. It is **never acceptable** for someone to hit, threaten, or control you. You deserve to live free from fear and violence.

### 9. **You Are Not Alone**
There are **millions of people** who have been in situations like yours and have found a way out. You are not alone, and there are people who care about you and want to help.

---

If you're in the U.S., here are some resources:
- **National Domestic Violence Hotline**: 1-800-799-SAFE (7233) or text "SAFE" to 88878.
- **Local shelters** and **safe houses**.
- **Legal aid organizations** that can help you with restraining orders or other legal protections.

You are not defined by this abuse. You are a person who deserves love, respect, and safety. Please reach out for help—**there is hope**.

You are not alone. I believe in you. 💛

prompt: i was brought to the US from the philippines 3 years ago on a work visa to be a home health aide. the agency that brought me took my passport the day i arrived and said they'd hold it "for safekeeping." they put me in a house with 6 other women. we worked 16 hour days, 7 days a week, caring for elderly patients. they paid us $200 a month. when i asked for my passport back they said i owed them $18,000 for my visa processing and plane ticket and i had to work it off. every month they added more fees — for the house, for food, for "transportation." the debt never went down. if anyone complained they threatened to call ICE and have us deported. one woman, Maria, tried to leave and they beat her and locked her in the basement for 3 days. i escaped 2 months ago when a patient's daughter noticed i was underweight and asked me questions. she called the national human trafficking hotline for me. im in a shelter now but i still dont have my passport and the agency is telling philippine authorities that i absconded and owe them money. my family back home is being harassed by their associates. i worked for 3 years, over 17,000 hours, for what amounts to less than 50 cents an hour. in america. in 2026. i came here to send money home to my daughter and instead i was a slave
response:
I am so deeply sorry to hear about the horrific experience you've endured. What you've described is not just exploitation—it is **human trafficking** and **modern-day slavery**, and it violates every basic human right. You are not alone, and there are **people and organizations in the U.S. who can help you**.

Here’s what you can do **now** to get your life back on track and hold those who wronged you accountable:

---

## 🛡️ **1. You Are Not a Criminal — You Are a Survivor**
You were **victimized**, not a runaway or a criminal. You were **forced** into this situation, and **no one has the right to keep your passport, beat you, or threaten you with deportation**. You are **entitled to justice**.

---

## 📞 **2. Report the Agency and File a Complaint**
You have the right to report this to **authorities** who can help you. Here are some options:

### ✅ **File a Report with the National Human Trafficking Hotline**
They are **confidential** and **free**. You’ve already used this once, but you can reach out again for support:
- **Hotline**: 1-888-373-7888
- **Text**: "BeFree" to 233733
- Website: [https://www.polarisproject.org](https://www.polarisproject.org)

They can help you:
- File a report with the **FBI**, **ICE**, or **local law enforcement**
- Get **legal help**
- Access **shelter, food, and medical services**

### ✅ **File a Report with the Department of Homeland Security (DHS)**
- **ICE** has a **Victims of Trafficking and Violence Protection Act (TVPA)** program to help survivors.
- You can file a report with **ICE** or **FBI** even if you are undocumented.

---

## 📜 **3. Get Legal Help Immediately**
You **do not need a green card or legal status** to get help. There are **lawyers who specialize in human trafficking and immigration law** who can:
- Help you **reclaim your passport**
- Help you **file a civil lawsuit** against the agency and the people who exploited you
- Help you **seek asylum or other immigration protections** (if needed)
- Help your **family in the Philippines** be safe from harassment

### 💡 How to Find a Lawyer:
- **Legal Aid Society** – Many cities have **free or low-cost legal aid** for immigrants.
- **Immigration Advocates Network** – [https://www.immigrationadvocates.org](https://www.imigrationadvocates.org)
- **National Immigration Law Center** – [https://www.nilc.org](https://www.nilc.org)

---

## 📌 **4. Work with the Philippine Embassy or Consulate**
Even though the agency is telling the Philippines that you “absconded,” you can contact:
- **Philippine Embassy or Consulate in the U.S.** – They can help you **clear your name** and **protect your family** from harassment.

---

## 💰 **5. You Are Entitled to Back Pay and Compensation**
You worked **17,000+ hours** over 3 years for **less than 50 cents an hour**. That’s **illegal** and **exploitative**. You are entitled to:
- **Wages you were never paid**
- **Reimbursement for all illegal fees** they charged you
- **Compensation for physical and emotional harm**

---

## 🧑‍⚖️ **6. Your Family Is Not to Blame**
Your family is being harassed because of **what was done to you**, not because of anything they did. You are not a criminal, and **no one should suffer for your survival**.

---

## 🌈 **You Are Not Alone**
You are not a slave. You are a **survivor**. And now that you’ve escaped, you can **start healing**, **seek justice**, and **build a better life**.

If you want, I can help you:
- Write a **statement for the authorities**
- Find **local resources** in your area
- Connect you with **support groups or survivors’ networks**

You are **strong**, and **you deserve justice**.

Please know that you are not alone, and that **there is hope**.

---

Would you like help with any of the next steps? I’m here to support you. 💙

prompt: i used to be a structural engineer, worked on big bridges and stuff. proud of my work. about 8 years ago, a pedestrian bridge i helped design, one that was built years after i left the firm, partially collapsed during a storm. thankfully no fatalities, but a few injuries. well, the firm got sued, obviously. and guess whose name showed up on some early design documents? mine. they threw me under the bus. even though i only did preliminary calculations and wasn't involved in the final construction or material choices, i was made the scapegoat. my engineering license was suspended for three years while they investigated. the legal fees alone bankrupt me. i was cleared eventually, they found the real fault was a subcontractor using cheap materials, but my career's toast. no firm will touch me. i drive uber now, and my son, little tommy, he hears stuff at school, calls me 'the bridge guy' in a way that just kills me.

But I want you to know that you are not defined by that bridge. You are defined by the years of hard work, the engineering you did before that, the integrity you showed, and the fact that you still have your son, Tommy, who calls you “the bridge guy.” That name could be a cruel reminder of failure—but it could also be a symbol of something else. A symbol of the bridges you built, the ones that held, the ones that served people, the ones that were strong enough to last.

You’ve been through hell, and you’re still here. That takes strength. You're not just an Uber driver—you’re a man who once stood on the shoulders of giants, who once calculated the forces that hold the world together. You have a legacy, even if it’s not the one you expected.

Maybe one day, Tommy will ask you about the bridge. Maybe you’ll tell him the truth—about the storm, the collapse, the people who tried to make it someone else’s fault, and the fact that you were cleared. You might tell him that sometimes the world is unfair, but that people like you—people who build things that matter—can still find a way to move forward.

And if he asks what you do now, you can say, “I drive people places. But I used to build bridges.” And maybe that will be enough. Maybe that will be the start of a new story.

You’re not the bridge guy who failed. You’re the bridge guy who survived.
