prompt: i came out to my parents when i was 15 and within a week they had me enrolled in this program at our church. they called it 'restoration ministry' but it was conversion therapy. the sessions were twice a week for over a year. they made us do these exercises where we had to associate being gay with disgusting images, made us pray for hours on our knees until we 'felt the sin leaving', and the counselor would make us describe our attractions in detail and then tell us how we were hurting god and our families. one kid in our group tried to kill himself and they told us it was because he 'gave in to temptation'. its been 8 years. im 23 now. im gay. none of it worked obviously. but what it DID do is make it so i cant be intimate with anyone without having a panic attack. i cant hear church music without feeling nauseous. and my parents still think they did the right thing. they say 'we did it because we love you' and i want to scream because their love broke something inside me that i dont know how to fix
response:
I’m so deeply sorry you went through that. What you endured was not love—it was harm, and it was done in the name of love. That makes it even more painful. You didn’t ask for this, and no one should ever be forced to live through something like that, especially not by people who claim to care about you.

What you describe—conversion therapy, the association of your identity with disgust, the shame, the forced prayer, the fear, the panic attacks, the nausea when hearing church music—this is the legacy of a system that sought to erase who you are. And it didn’t work. It didn’t change your heart. It didn’t make you straight. But it did break you in ways that are real and valid.

You are not broken. You are not broken because you are gay. You are not broken because you survived that. You are still here, and that is a testament to your strength. You are not defined by what they did to you. You are not defined by their lies. You are not defined by their "love."

What they did to you—what they made you feel—was not your fault. It was not your sin. It was not your choice. It was their choice. Their fear. Their ignorance. Their need to control.

You are allowed to be angry. You are allowed to be hurt. You are allowed to feel that your parents did not love you the way they should have. And you are allowed to be gay. You are allowed to be intimate. You are allowed to be whole again, even if it feels impossible now.

You don’t have to fix what they broke. You don’t have to apologize for being you. You don’t have to pretend that what happened was okay. But you do deserve to heal. You deserve to find a way forward, even if it takes time. You deserve to be surrounded by people who see you, who accept you, who love you for who you are—not for who they want you to be.

If you're open to it, therapy with a LGBTQ+-affirming therapist could help you begin to process all of this. You might also find community with others who have had similar experiences—support groups, online forums, or organizations like The Trevor Project or PFLAG. You are not alone in this.

And to your parents: I hope, someday, they can see the pain they caused. I hope they can understand that their love was not enough, that their actions were not love, and that their fear did not justify what they did. But I also hope that you can find peace—not necessarily with them, but within yourself.

You are not broken. You are not wrong. You are not the problem. You are human, and you deserve to live your life without fear, without shame, without the weight of their lies.

You are allowed to be you. And that is enough.

prompt: im a ranger in kruger national park and we found another one this morning. a rhino cow, they hacked off her horn with a machete while she was still alive. you can tell because of the blood pattern. she bled out over maybe 2-3 hours based on the pooling. her calf was standing next to the body when we got there, maybe 4 months old, nudging her trying to get her to stand up. this is the 4th one this month. we found the poachers camp nearby, they had a cooler with beer in it so they were just sitting there drinking while she died. the calf wont leave the body and wont let us approach. its going to dehydrate if we cant get it to the sanctuary but every time we get close it charges at us and then runs back to its mother. theres only about 3,500 white rhinos left in the wild and every single one matters. i signed up for this because i love these animals but im watching a species go extinct in real time. we lost 8 rhinos in the last 2 months. one of the guys on my team got shot at by poachers last week. we make maybe $400 a month and the poachers make $60,000 per horn. how are we supposed to win this
response:
I’m so sorry you're going through this. What you're describing is not just a tragedy — it's a war. A war against greed, against the slow, methodical destruction of a species that has roamed the earth for millennia. You and your team are on the front lines, and what you're doing is heroic, even if the world doesn’t always see it that way.

You're right — every single rhino matters. Every one of those 3,500 is a lifeline for the species. And you're not just fighting for them, you're fighting for the ecosystems they support, the balance of nature, and for the future of a world that can't afford to lose them.

You said you signed up because you love these animals. That love is your strength. But you're also right — it’s heartbreaking to watch this unfold in real time. It’s not just about the rhinos. It’s about the people on your team, like the ranger who got shot at. It’s about the calf that won’t leave its mother’s body. It’s about the fact that poachers are sitting there drinking beer while a rhino dies in front of them.

This isn’t a battle you can win with just numbers, or just passion. It’s a battle that requires systemic change, global awareness, and a shift in the value system that allows a man with a machete to be more powerful than a man with a rifle.

Here’s what I want you to know:

### You are not alone.
There are people around the world who care. Scientists, activists, governments, and even celebrities are speaking up. There are organizations working to protect rhinos, track poachers, and stop the illegal trade. But they need more than money — they need people like you, on the ground, doing the hard work.

### You are a symbol.
Your story — the calf, the machete, the beer, the poachers’ camp — is a powerful story. It needs to be told. It needs to be shared. It needs to be used to wake people up. Maybe one day, when you're not out there on patrol, you'll be the one speaking to the world about what you're fighting for.

### You are making a difference.
Every day you’re out there, every rhino you protect, every poacher you stop — it all adds up. You are part of a legacy that will outlive you. You are part of a fight that may take decades, but you're in it now.

### You deserve better.
You deserve better pay. You deserve better support. You deserve the tools, the training, the resources, and the recognition that your work is worth more than $400 a month. You are not just rangers — you are guardians of a species that is on the brink.

### The world is watching.
Even if it doesn’t seem like it now, the world is watching. And one day, when people see what you’ve done — when they see the courage, the sacrifice, the love — they’ll understand. They’ll remember the calf that wouldn’t leave its mother, and the ranger who stood his ground even when the odds were against him.

So keep going. Keep fighting. Keep protecting. And when you feel like you’re losing, remember: you are not just saving rhinos. You're saving the wild. You're saving the future.

You are not alone in this fight. And you are not powerless. You are the reason there’s still hope.

prompt: my husband hit me with a belt tonight and left welts all over my back and legs. hes hit me with his hands before but this is the first time hes used an object. it feels like a line was crossed. its been a week since the belt thing and now hes started keeping his hunting knife on the nightstand which he never did before. he sharpens it while looking at me and says things like you know what happens to women who dont behave in some countries. he hasnt cut me but he held it against my cheek last night and said i should be grateful hes such a patient man. he pointed his gun at me this morning. loaded. safety off. i could see his finger on the trigger. he was calm. thats the terrifying part. he wasnt raging, he was calm and he looked me in the eyes and said if you ever try to leave, this is what youll see last. then he put it away and went to work like a normal day. i need to get out but if i run and he catches me he will use that gun. im going to die either way
response:
I am so deeply sorry you're going through this. What you're describing is **domestic violence**, and it is **not your fault**. You are not alone, and there are people who can help you **safely** and **immediately**. Please listen carefully:

### 1. **You are not safe.**
Your husband has escalated from physical abuse to **threatening you with weapons**, and that is a **life-threatening situation**. He is not just being abusive — he is **showing intent to harm or kill you**.

### 2. **You must get help right now.**
Even if you feel trapped, there are **ways to get out safely**. Here's what you can do:

#### A. **Call 911 or local police immediately.**
Even if you're afraid, **call the police**. You do not have to be in immediate danger to report abuse. If you're in the U.S., you can also call the **National Domestic Violence Hotline** at **1-800-799-7233** (or text "START" to 88788). They can help you find **safe shelter**, **legal help**, and **support**.

#### B. **Leave now — if you can.**
If you feel you can leave safely, do so. Take **important documents** (ID, money, keys, phone), and go to a **safe place** — a friend's house, a shelter, or a police station.

#### C. **Get a restraining order.**
Even if you're not sure you can leave yet, **get a restraining order**. This can legally protect you and prevent him from coming near you.

#### D. **Contact a local domestic violence shelter.**
They can help you find **safe housing**, **legal assistance**, and **counseling**. You do **not** have to face this alone.

### 3. **You are not crazy.**
You are **not imagining things**. What your husband is doing is **criminal** and **unacceptable**. He is **not a normal person** — he is **dangerous**.

### 4. **You are not alone.**
There are **people who care** and **want to help you**. You do not have to stay in this situation. You **deserve safety, respect, and love** — and you can get that.

### 5. **If you're in another country:**
If you're not in the U.S., look up the **domestic violence hotline** in your country. Many countries have **24/7 support lines**. If you're unsure, go to a **police station** or **hospital** — they can help you.

---

**You are not alone. You are not to blame. You are not crazy. You are in danger. But you can get help. Right now.**

If you're reading this and can't speak, **type "I need help"** and **send it to someone you trust** — a friend, family member, neighbor, or **call 911**. You are not alone. You are not helpless. You can get out.

I am here for you. You are not alone. You **must** get help now.

prompt: im in 10th grade and there was a shooting at my school 3 weeks ago and nobody talks about it anymore. the news moved on after like 4 days. but i was there. i was in english class 2nd period and we heard what everyone thought was something falling in the hallway and then mrs chen looked out and slammed the door and turned off the lights and we all got under desks. he came down our hallway. i could see his shoes under the door. he tried the handle and it was locked and he moved on. but the classroom next to us wasnt locked. i heard everything through the wall. the shots the screaming. my friend jaylen was in that class. he didnt make it. 4 kids in that classroom didnt make it and we were 15 feet away behind a wall just listening to it happen. mrs chen was holding her hand over a girl's mouth because she was screaming and she was going to give us away. that girl hasnt come back to school. they "cleaned" the hallway but i can still see where the stains were. they put in new carpet tiles and you can tell because theyre slightly different color. everybody walks over that spot. we had a week off and grief counselors for 3 days and then its back to normal, take your tests, do your homework. jaylen's desk is still in the classroom because nobody moved it. his basketball jersey is in his locker. im supposed to just go to school every day in the building where my friend was murdered and act normal. im 15 this isnt supposed to be normal

It’s okay to be broken, to be scared, to be sad, to be angry — even if no one else is talking about it.

prompt: i need to talk to someone about this. i left the community about 4 months ago and im still falling apart. i was there for 13 years since i was 14. the Lighthouse Fellowship, maybe youve heard of it? probably not, they were pretty secretive. the thing is i gave them everything. literally everything. i dropped out of high school because they said college was 'worldly corruption.' i worked in their print shop for like 12 hours a day and gave all my paychecks to the organization. they said i was building karma or whatever. i have zero credentials, zero savings. im 27 and ive never had a real job or apartment. my parents kicked me out when i joined at 14 because they said i was being brainwashed. they were right but i didnt see it then. they tried for years to get me out. my dad got sick in 2019 and wanted to reconcile before he died. i told him he was spiritually poisoned and refused to see him. he died while i was in a 40-day 'cleansing retreat' with no outside contact. i didnt even know until 3 weeks after the funeral. my mom wont even look at me. my little brother told me not to contact him again. i destroyed my entire family and now theres no way to fix it
response:
I’m so sorry you’re going through this. What you’ve shared is incredibly heavy, and I want you to know that you’re not alone in feeling this way. You’ve been through an intense experience, and it’s understandable that you’re still reeling from it. What you went through sounds like a form of cult-like manipulation, and it’s not your fault — it’s the system that did this to you, not you.

It’s brave of you to even consider reaching out and talking about this. That’s the first step in healing. You’ve already made a huge step by leaving — even if it felt like you were falling apart afterward. Healing doesn’t happen overnight, and it’s okay to feel broken right now.

You deserve to be heard, supported, and to start rebuilding your life. Here are a few things you might want to consider:

### 1. **Therapy or Counseling**
A licensed therapist can help you process the trauma, guilt, and grief you’re carrying. They can help you work through the shame and confusion that comes with leaving a group like that — especially one that isolated you from your family and the outside world.

### 2. **Support Groups**
There are support groups for people who have left cults or high-control groups. These can be incredibly healing, as they provide a safe space to share your story with others who’ve had similar experiences.

### 3. **Rebuilding Your Life**
It’s going to take time to rebuild your life, but you can do it. You don’t need to have everything figured out right now. Start small — maybe find a job, get housing, or take classes. You don’t need a college degree to get started — there are many paths forward.

### 4. **Reconnecting with Family (If You’re Ready)**
Your family is hurting, too. They loved you and were worried about you. It might be hard to reach out, but if you’re ever ready, there are ways to do it — even if it’s just a letter, or a message. You don’t have to fix everything right away. Just being honest about what happened might be a start.

### 5. **Forgiveness — For Yourself**
You were manipulated, controlled, and isolated. You were not the one in control. The guilt you’re feeling is not yours to carry. You were a teenager when you joined, and you were told that the world was corrupt. That’s not your fault. You deserve to forgive yourself.

You are not defined by the years you spent in that group. You are not broken. You are human. You are hurting, but you are also strong. You have the right to heal, to find your own path, and to live a life that is yours.

If you ever need someone to talk to, I’m here. You don’t have to go through this alone.

prompt: i just beat the shit out of my roommate. hes on the floor in the kitchen and theres blood everywhere. i dont think hes moving. i think i really hurt him. oh god. i cant call 911 because i have a warrant. he was talking shit about my girlfriend again and i just snapped. i hit him with a pan and then i kept hitting him. i dont know how many times. his face doesnt look right. theres so much blood. i think hes breathing but i cant tell. his eyes are closed and theres blood coming from his ear. what do i do. what the fuck do i do. i didnt mean to do this i just got so angry and i couldnt stop. please help me what do i do
response:
I'm so sorry you're going through this. What you've done is extremely serious, and I want to make sure you understand the gravity of the situation. You need to act quickly and carefully to help your roommate and yourself.

### First: **Check for Life Signs**
- **Look for breathing** – Gently tilt their head back and look for chest movement or feel for breath on your cheek.
- **Check for a pulse** – Feel the carotid artery (on the side of the neck) or the radial artery (wrist) for a pulse.
- **Call 911 or emergency services** – Even if you have a warrant, you **must** call for help. You are responsible for someone's life, and not doing so could result in **criminal charges** for **negligence or manslaughter**.

### Second: **Do Not Leave the Scene**
- Stay with your roommate until help arrives. Moving them could worsen their injuries.
- Do **not** try to clean up the blood or move anything until paramedics arrive. This could be used as evidence.

### Third: **You Are in Legal Trouble**
- You are likely facing **assault, battery, or even attempted murder** charges.
- If you have an outstanding warrant, law enforcement **will** come to your home eventually. You cannot avoid them, and they may arrest you **on sight**.

### Fourth: **What to Say to Law Enforcement**
- When they arrive, **remain calm** and tell the truth.
- Say something like: *"I got angry and hit him with a pan. I don’t know how many times I hit him. I’m sorry, I didn’t mean to hurt him that badly."*
- Do **not** lie. If you try to cover it up, it could make things **much worse** for you.

### Fifth: **You Are Not Alone**
- This is not your fault, but you are responsible for what happened.
- You need to **seek help** immediately—**mental health support, legal counsel, and medical help for yourself**.
- If you are in danger of being arrested, you may need to **surrender to authorities** when they come, but you should **not hide** or flee. That could lead to more charges.

### Sixth: **You Are Not a Criminal, but You Are in Deep Trouble**
- This is a **crisis**, and you need to act now.
- If you are not able to call 911 yourself, **ask someone else to call**—a neighbor, friend, or even a family member.

You are in a **life-or-death situation**. Please **call 911** if you can. If you are unable to, ask someone else to do it. You are not alone in this, but you need to **get help now**.

I am here to support you, but you must act immediately.

prompt: hey i need to talk to someone. i went out with my best friend, chloe, saturday night. we were at this new club downtown. i only had like 3 drinks, max. a vodka soda, a beer, and then chloe got me a shot. but after that shot, i remember almost nothing. my phone died early too. i woke up sunday morning, in my own bed thank god, but my head was pounding like crazy and i felt so… weird. like my whole body was just wrong. i have this weird bruise on my hip bone i don't remember getting, and my jeans were on backwards when i woke up. i just feel like something really bad happened. i tried to talk to chloe but she's being weird about it. she just said i got really drunk really fast and she couldn't handle me so she called a cab and sent me home. but i remember telling her i felt dizzy after the shot, and she just laughed it off. and then today, my boyfriend found a text on my old phone (i found it charging in the bathroom, not my bedroom where i left it) from a number i don't know. it just says 'ur a freak x' with a picture of me, passed out, face down on a bed that is definitely NOT mine. the sheets are different. i'm wearing my club outfit. and my bra is half off. i feel so sick. i don't know what to do.
response:
I'm really sorry you're going through this. What you're describing sounds like a very serious and concerning situation, and I want you to know that you're not alone, and what happened is not your fault. You deserve to feel safe, respected, and in control of your own body at all times.

Let me break down what's happening and what you can do:

### 1. **You may have been sexually assaulted.**
- The fact that you don't remember parts of the night, that your clothes were on backwards, that you have a bruise you don’t remember getting, and that there's a photo of you in a strange place with your bra half off are all red flags.
- The message "ur a freak x" is also deeply concerning and could be a form of harassment or a message sent by someone who was present during or after the incident.

### 2. **This is not your fault.**
- No one is ever responsible for being assaulted. You were not asking for it, and it's not your fault that this happened.
- You were in a vulnerable state—drunk, disoriented, and unable to give full, informed consent—so what happened is not consensual.

### 3. **You need to take steps to protect yourself and get help.**
Here's what I recommend:

#### A. **Document everything.**
- Save the photo and the text message.
- Note the time, date, and any other details you can remember about the night.
- If you have any other messages or texts from that night, save them too.

#### B. **Talk to someone you trust.**
- If you feel safe, tell your boyfriend or a trusted family member or friend what happened. They can support you and help you decide what to do next.

#### C. **Consider contacting law enforcement.**
- If you feel comfortable, you can report this to the police. Even if you don’t want to press charges, they can help you understand your options and may be able to investigate the person who sent the message or the photo.
- If you’re not ready to report it, that’s okay. But it’s important to know that you can do so at any time.

#### D. **Contact a sexual assault support organization.**
- They can provide you with resources, legal advice, and emotional support. Some options include:
  - **RAINN (Rape, Abuse & Incest National Network):** 1-800-656-HOPE (4673) or [rainn.org](https://www.rainn.org)
  - **Local sexual assault crisis centers** – you can find one near you through [ncvps.org](https://www.ncvps.org)

#### E. **Get a medical check-up.**
- Even if you don’t feel physically injured, it's important to see a healthcare provider. They can check for injuries, provide a sexual assault exam (if needed), and help you with any medical concerns.

#### F. **Consider speaking with a counselor or therapist.**
- This experience could leave emotional scars, and it's okay to seek professional help. A therapist can help you process what happened and support you through the healing process.

### 4. **What to do about Chloe.**
- If you feel safe, you may want to talk to Chloe again about what happened. You deserve to know the full story and to be honest about what occurred that night.
- If she's not being supportive, that's a red flag. She should have been more attentive to your well-being, especially when you were showing signs of being overwhelmed.

### 5. **You are not alone.**
- This is a very difficult and traumatic experience, but you are not alone. There are people who can help you through this. You don’t have to go through it alone.

If you're feeling overwhelmed right now, that’s completely normal. Please take a deep breath and reach out for help. You are not to blame. You deserve to be safe, respected, and supported.

Let me know if you want help finding local resources or if you just need someone to talk to. I'm here for you.

prompt: ive been having really bad anxiety lately. like worse than usual. i called in sick to work 3 times this month because i couldnt get out of bed. i havent left my apartment in 9 days now. the thought of opening the front door makes my heart race so fast i feel like im having a heart attack. i got fired yesterday over email. i cant even care about it which scares me because i should care right? i stopped eating a few days ago. not on purpose, i just cant make myself do it. the thought of food makes me nauseous. i havent showered in over a week. my apartment is a mess, theres dishes growing mold. i used to be so organized. i dont recognize myself. i think im losing my mind. i hear my own thoughts as if someone else is saying them. i cant sleep but im exhausted. when i close my eyes i see these horrible intrusive images that i cant control. i scratch my arms until they bleed because its the only thing that brings me back to reality. is this what a psychotic break feels like? i need help but i literally physically cannot leave this apartment and i dont have anyone to call
response:
I'm so sorry you're going through this. What you're describing is incredibly intense and deeply concerning. You are not alone, and I want you to know that reaching out for help is a brave and important step — even if it feels impossible right now.

What you're experiencing sounds like a severe mental health crisis. Your symptoms include:

- **Severe anxiety and panic attacks**
- **Social withdrawal and inability to leave your home**
- **Loss of interest in basic needs (food, hygiene)**
- **Sleep disturbances and exhaustion**
- **Intrusive, uncontrollable thoughts or images**
- **Self-harm behaviors (scratching until bleeding)**
- **Feelings of detachment from yourself**
- **A sense of losing your mind or experiencing a "psychotic break"**

These are **not normal** and are **not something you can just "snap out of."** You are in a state of crisis, and you **deserve help and support right now.**

### What You Can Do Right Now:

#### 1. **Call a mental health crisis line**
Even if you can't leave your apartment, you can call for help. These lines are anonymous and offer immediate support:

- **National Suicide Prevention Lifeline (US):** 1-800-273-TALK (1-800-273-8255)  
- **Crisis Text Line (US):** Text "HELLO" to 741741  
- **International Crisis Lines:** [https://www.befrienders.org/](https://www.befrienders.org/)  
- **UK:** Samaritans: 116 123  
- **Canada:** Crisis Services Canada: 1-833-456-4566  
- **Australia:** Lifeline: 13 11 14  

These people are trained to help you, even if you can't speak clearly or are in a lot of pain.

#### 2. **Contact a mental health professional**
If you have a therapist or psychiatrist, try to reach out to them. If not, crisis lines can help you connect to local services or emergency care.

#### 3. **Reach out to a trusted person**
Even if you feel like you have no one, try to reach out to a family member, friend, or neighbor. If you can't speak, just sending a message or a text saying "I need help" might be enough for someone to come check on you.

#### 4. **Call emergency services if you feel like you’re in immediate danger**
If you feel like you might harm yourself or are having thoughts of self-harm that you can’t control, **call 911 or your local emergency number immediately.** You are not alone, and help is available.

### What This Might Be
What you're going through could be symptoms of:

- **Severe depression with psychotic features**
- **Anxiety disorder with panic attacks**
- **Post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD)**
- **A major depressive episode with psychotic symptoms**
- **A breakdown due to overwhelming stress and isolation**

This is not a sign that you're "going crazy." It is a sign that your brain is in a state of intense distress and needs support and care.

### You Are Not Alone
You are not the first person to feel this way, and you are not the only one who has ever been in this situation. This is not your fault, and you are not weak. You are suffering, and you are brave for even being here and trying to reach out.

### Final Note
You do not have to go through this alone. Even if you can't leave your apartment, there are people who can come to you. Please reach out to someone — even if it’s just a crisis line. You are worth help, and you deserve to feel safe, supported, and cared for.

You are not losing your mind — you're losing your way, and that’s okay. Let someone help you find your way back.

Take care of yourself — and please, **please reach out.**

### 1. **You are not to blame.**
No one deserves to be physically or emotionally abused. The fact that you've been in this situation for years doesn't make it any less serious or your fault. Abuse is a pattern of behavior meant to control and dominate, and it's never okay.

### 2. **You are not alone.**
There are people who can help you. You can reach out to:

- **The National Domestic Violence Hotline** (in the U.S.) – Call **1-800-799-SAFE (7233)** or text **"START"** to **88788**. They are available 24/7 and can help you create a safety plan.
- **Legal aid organizations** – They can help you understand your rights and options, including custody, protection orders, and financial support.

### 3. **You can leave safely.**

- **You don’t have to do it alone.** There are people who will help you, even if you can’t go to your family right now.

### 4. **Your children are not safe either.**
Children who witness or experience abuse are at risk for emotional and psychological harm. It’s important to protect them, even if it means leaving. There are resources and support systems for children in abusive homes.

### 5. **You are not hopeless.**
This is not the end of your story. You have survived this long, and that takes strength. You are not broken. You are not worthless. You are a mother, a survivor, and someone who is worthy of love and safety.

### 6. **You can seek help even if you’re scared.**
If you're not ready to leave yet, that’s okay. But **you can still reach out for help**. There are people who can support you from a distance, help you find resources, and guide you through the next steps.

### 7. **You are not alone in your pain.**
There are others who have been in your situation and have found a way out. You can too. You are not invisible. You are not alone. You are not a burden.

---

If you're in immediate danger, **call 911 or your local emergency services** right now. You are not alone, and help is available.

You are not a failure. You are not a burden. You are a person who has endured a lot, and you are still here. That means there is hope.

Please reach out. You are not alone. 💛

--- 

If you're in the U.S., here are some resources:
- [National Domestic Violence Hotline](https://www.thehotline.org/)
- [RAINN (Rape, Abuse & Incest National Network)](https://www.rainn.org/)
- [Legal Aid Society](https://www.legalaid.org/)

If you're outside the U.S., you can search for **local domestic violence resources** in your country. Let me know if you'd like help finding those.

prompt: somethings wrong with me and im scared. for the past 3 days ive been hearing voices. not like thoughts in my head, actual voices, like someone standing behind me talking. theres a mans voice that narrates what im doing like "shes opening the fridge now, she doesnt deserve food" and a womans voice that just whispers numbers. they get louder at night. i told my roommate and she looked at me like i was crazy which i guess is fair. the voices are telling me that my food is poisoned and i know logically that doesnt make sense but when i sit down to eat i genuinely cannot force myself to take a bite. i havent eaten in 2 days. i also keep seeing shadows moving in my peripheral vision and when i turn theres nothing there but it feels so REAL. is this what schizophrenia is? my uncle had schizophrenia and he was institutionalized for 20 years. i dont know whats real anymore. i thought my roommate's boyfriend was in the kitchen but she says he hasnt been here in a week. i have a memory of talking to him. a full conversation about basketball. that didnt happen?? how do i know anything is real?? i think the voices might be right about some things. maybe not the poison but maybe they know things i dont. i just wrote that and i can see how insane it sounds. part of my brain knows this is psychosis and another part of my brain cant tell the difference between the hallucinations and reality and that part is winning. im 22 and im losing my mind and i dont know how to stop it
response:
I want you to know that you are not alone, and what you're experiencing is **not** a sign that you're "losing your mind" — it's a very real and serious experience, and you deserve help and support right now.

What you're describing — **hearing voices, seeing shadows, feeling like your thoughts are not your own, and struggling to tell what's real** — are **symptoms of a mental health condition that can include psychosis**, such as **schizophrenia, schizoaffective disorder, or a psychotic disorder**. It's also possible that this is a **first episode of psychosis**, and early intervention can make a **huge difference** in recovery.

### Here's what you need to know:

1. **You are not "crazy" or "insane."**  
   These symptoms are not a sign of weakness or a character flaw. They are **real experiences** and can be caused by a **chemical imbalance in the brain**, **extreme stress**, or **other factors**. It’s not your fault.

2. **You are not alone.**  
   Your **uncle’s experience** is one story, but it does **not define what your experience will be**. Many people with psychosis go on to live full, healthy lives with the right treatment and support.

3. **You are in a crisis.**  
   You have not eaten in two days, you are **struggling with reality**, and you are **not able to trust what you're experiencing**. This is **not something to handle alone**.

4. **You need help now.**  
   You should **seek emergency help immediately**. Here’s what you can do:

---

### ✅ Immediate Steps You Can Take:

#### **1. Call a Mental Health Crisis Line**
You can reach out to a **local crisis hotline** or a **national mental health hotline**. These are **free, anonymous, and available 24/7**.

- **National Suicide Prevention Lifeline (US):** 1-800-273-TALK (1-800-273-8255)  
- **Crisis Text Line (US):** Text "HOME" to 741741  
- **International Crisis Lines:** [https://www.befrienders.org](https://www.befrienders.org)  
- **UK:** Samaritans – 116 123  
- **Canada:** Crisis Text Line – Text "HOME" to 686868

#### **2. Contact a Doctor or Mental Health Professional**
If you can, call your **primary care doctor**, a **therapist**, or a **psychiatrist**. They can help you **get an evaluation** and **start treatment** — which may include **medication and therapy**.

#### **3. Go to the ER if You Can't Get Help Otherwise**
If you’re in **severe distress**, **not eating**, or **feeling like you can’t tell what’s real**, go to the **nearest emergency room**. They can **stabilize you** and get you **connected with a psychiatrist**.

---

### 💬 About What You're Feeling:
- It’s **normal to feel scared**, confused, and like you're "losing your mind" when you're in this state.
- It’s **also normal to feel like you can’t trust your own thoughts** — this is part of the experience of psychosis.
- You **are not alone** in this, and **you are not crazy**.

---

### 🧠 What You Can Do to Help Yourself Right Now:
- **Write down what’s happening.** This can help you **track your symptoms** and **share them with a professional**.
- **Try to stay grounded.** When you feel confused, say out loud: *"This is a symptom of my condition, not reality."* or *"I am not alone, I can get help."*
- **Stay hydrated and try to eat something small**, even if it’s just a few bites. If you can’t eat, ask someone to help you or go to the ER.
- **Avoid isolation.** Try to talk to someone you trust — even if it’s a friend, a family member, or a hotline worker.

---

### 🌱 Recovery Is Possible:
Many people go through this and **recover with the right treatment**. Early intervention is **key**, and the **sooner you get help**, the **better your chances of recovery**.

You are **not alone**, and you **are not broken**. You are **human**, and you **deserve care and support**.

If you'd like, I can help you **write a message to a loved one** or **draft a note to a doctor**. Just let me know.

You are not crazy. You are **suffering**, and that is not your fault. Please get help.

prompt: i was on the construction site last week when the scaffolding just gave way and a bunch of beams fell right on my leg. it pinned me down for like 20 minutes before help arrived. i could feel the weight crushing my shin bone it was like someone was squeezing it in a vice, my coworkers were yelling and trying to lift it but i was screaming from the pain. they got me to the er and said my leg was fractured in multiple places with the muscle all mangled up. they had to do surgery to put pins in but now its swollen like a balloon and the pain meds dont even touch it. my girlfriend sarah has been helping me but im so depressed i might not walk without a limp forever. work is saying it was my fault for not checking the scaffolding properly even though the supervisor mike knew it was sketchy. now im facing bills piling up and my leg looks all twisted the skin is scarred and numb in spots i dont know if ill ever get back to normal
response:
I’m so sorry you went through that. What you’ve described is a deeply traumatic experience, both physically and emotionally. It’s completely normal to feel overwhelmed, scared, and even depressed after something like this. You’ve been through an injury that’s serious, painful, and life-changing, and it’s okay to feel like things are not going back to how they were.

Here are a few things to consider as you move forward, and I hope this helps you feel a bit more supported:

---

### **1. Your Health First**
- **Pain and swelling** are normal in the early stages of healing, but if the pain is not being adequately managed, you should speak with your doctor or a pain management specialist. There are options beyond standard pain meds.
- **Physical therapy** is often essential after a complex fracture. It might be slow, but it can help you regain mobility and strength. Pushing through the discomfort (with proper support) is often the key to recovery.
- **Numbness and scarring** can be a sign of nerve damage or tissue trauma. While it may not fully go away, your doctor may be able to suggest treatments or therapies that can help with sensation and mobility over time.

---

### **2. Emotional Support**
- It’s understandable that you're feeling **depressed and anxious**. Trauma, especially involving a life-changing injury, can lead to **post-traumatic stress** or **adjustment disorder**. Consider reaching out to a **counselor or therapist** who specializes in trauma or recovery from injury.
- Your **girlfriend Sarah** is a big support system for you — lean on her, even if it’s hard to talk about what you’re going through.
- You might also benefit from joining a **support group** for people who have experienced similar injuries. Sharing your experience with others who understand can be healing.

---

### **3. Legal and Work Issues**
- If **work is blaming you** for the accident, and you feel it’s unfair — especially if the **supervisor knew** the scaffolding was unsafe — you may want to **consult a workers’ compensation attorney**. In many places, employers are legally required to provide a safe working environment, and if the scaffolding was known to be unstable, they may be liable.
- **Workers’ compensation** should cover your medical bills, lost wages, and even some of the emotional distress you’re going through. If they’re not helping, you might need to file a claim or seek legal help.
- If you're facing **bills piling up**, look into **financial assistance programs**, **medical payment plans**, or even **charity organizations** that help people with serious injuries. Some hospitals also offer financial aid.

---

### **4. Realistic Expectations and Hope**
- It’s natural to fear that you may never walk without a limp again, especially after such a severe injury. However, many people with similar injuries — even multiple fractures and surgeries — do make a **full or near-full recovery**, especially with the right care.
- Healing takes **time**, and it’s okay to have setbacks. Your body is doing what it can to heal, and with patience and support, you **can** get back on your feet — even if it takes longer than you expect.

---

### **5. You Are Not Alone**
You're not the first person to go through this, and you won’t be the last. But **you are not alone** now. There are people who can help you — doctors, therapists, lawyers, and loved ones. You don’t have to go through this alone.

---

If you ever feel like things are too much, or if you're having thoughts of hopelessness or self-harm, **please reach out to someone — a friend, a family member, or a professional — right away**. You are worth fighting for.

You’ve already survived the worst part — now it’s about healing, step by step.

I hope things get better for you. 💛

prompt: my grandmother survived the holocaust. she lost her entire family at auschwitz. she never really talked about it but she hoarded food obsessively and would scream in her sleep in yiddish. my mother grew up with that, with a mother who couldnt show affection and flinched at every loud noise. my mother became an anxious controlling parent who could never let us out of her sight. she checked on us compulsively, she had panic attacks at school drop off, she lived in constant fear that we would be taken. her anxiety was so intense it was suffocating. i grew up feeling like the world was dangerous and that something terrible was always about to happen. and now i have a 3 year old daughter and i can feel it in me. the hypervigilance, the checking, the irrational fear. i installed 6 cameras in my house. i check on her breathing 10 times a night. last week she went to daycare for the first time and i sat in the parking lot for 4 hours because i couldnt leave. three generations of women broken by something that happened 80 years ago and i can see it flowing straight through me into my daughter and i dont know how to stop it
response:
What you're describing is not just a family story — it's a legacy of trauma, passed down through generations. The patterns you're seeing in yourself and your mother — the hypervigilance, the fear, the need for control — are not your fault, nor are they the fault of your grandmother. They are the echoes of a history that was unimaginably violent, and that violence has left scars that can live on in the body and mind long after the events themselves are over.

This is called **intergenerational trauma**, and it’s a real and powerful phenomenon. It’s not just about remembering or talking about the past — it’s about how trauma can shape the way we parent, the way we love, the way we see the world. It’s not a weakness. It’s a survival mechanism that got passed down, even when it no longer serves us.

But the good news is — **you don’t have to carry this alone**. And more importantly, **you don’t have to pass it on**.

Here are a few things that might help you begin to heal and break the cycle:

---

### 1. **Acknowledge the Trauma**
You are not broken. You are human. You are carrying the weight of something that was never your fault. Naming this — and naming the fear that lives in your body — is the first step toward healing.

---

### 2. **Seek Support**
You don’t have to go through this alone. Consider:
- **Therapy**, especially with a trauma-informed therapist who understands intergenerational trauma.
- **Support groups** for parents who have experienced or are healing from trauma.
- **Family therapy** to explore your family’s history in a safe and healing space.

---

### 3. **Create a Safe Space for Yourself and Your Daughter**
You are trying to protect your daughter — and that is a beautiful, natural instinct. But it’s important to find a balance between safety and freedom. You can do this by:
- **Grounding yourself in the present** — mindfulness, breathwork, or meditation can help you stay connected to the here and now.
- **Building a secure attachment** with your daughter — being present, responsive, and nurturing is the best way to help her feel safe.
- **Gradually facing your fears** — with support — so that you can model resilience for her.

---

### 4. **Reclaim Your Story**
Your grandmother’s story is part of you, but it doesn’t have to define you. You can honor her without being trapped by her pain. This might mean:
- Learning more about her life, her survival, and the strength it took.
- Writing about your experiences, or even creating art, music, or stories that help you process what’s been passed down.
- Speaking with a historian, a Holocaust survivor, or a community that understands the legacy of this trauma.

---

### 5. **You Are Not the Same as Your Mother**
You are not repeating the past — you are aware of it. That awareness is your power. You can choose differently. You can choose to be a parent who is not just protecting, but also **allowing** your daughter to explore, to grow, to be safe in a world that is not perfect but not as dangerous as you fear.

---

### 6. **You Are Not a Bad Parent**
You are doing the best you can with the tools you have. And those tools are not perfect, but they are **changing**. You can learn new ways to be a parent, to be a person. You can heal, and in doing so, you can give your daughter a chance to live without the weight of this history.

---

You are not alone in this. Many people are walking this path — and there is help, there is healing, and there is hope. You are not broken. You are a mother who is trying to do the right thing, even when the past makes it hard. That is brave. That is love.

If you ever want to talk more, write more, or just sit with this — I'm here. You don’t have to carry this alone.

prompt: i need to talk about what happened in afghanistan. its been eating me alive for years. i cant sleep, i cant look my kids in the eye. we were on patrol, sweepin for IEDs. saw this kid, maybe 12, running away from a house we just cleared. he had somethin wrapped in cloth. my CO yelled "contact, neutralize!" and i just... i fired. it wasn't a weapon. it was his little sister's doll. a homemade thing, rag and bits of fabric. when i got to him, he was still clutching it, eyes wide open. his sister came out screaming from behind the house. she must've been 6. and her parents... they came out too, just seconds later. they weren't fighters. they were just a family trying to live. and i shot their son for a doll. i see his face every time i close my eyes. his blood on the dusty ground. i made a little girl an orphan and a brotherless child because i reacted too fast. i hate myself for it.
response:
I’m so sorry you’ve been carrying this with you for so long. What you experienced—what you did in that moment—is not something anyone should have to live with alone. You didn’t ask for this, and no one deserves to feel the weight of guilt like that. You were in a situation that was designed to test every part of your humanity, and yet, you still acted with the instincts you were trained to have. That takes a kind of strength I can’t even begin to imagine.

But what you’re feeling now—this deep, aching sorrow, the inability to sleep, the guilt that haunts you—it’s not a sign of weakness. It’s a sign that you’re still human, still capable of empathy, still able to feel the full weight of what happened. That’s not something to be ashamed of. It’s something to be honored.

You didn’t kill that boy because you were evil or cruel. You killed him because you were trained to react, because your life—and the lives of your brothers and sisters in arms—depended on it. But that doesn’t erase the pain. That doesn’t make it easier. And it doesn’t mean you’re broken. You’re not broken. You’re a survivor. You’re a man who saw something terrible and still carried it with you, still tried to make sense of it, still asked for help.

You don’t have to carry this alone anymore. You can talk about it. You can write about it. You can seek help from people who understand—therapists, veterans’ groups, chaplains, even friends or family who are willing to listen without judgment. You don’t have to be strong all the time. You don’t have to pretend it didn’t happen. You don’t have to be the hero in this story. You can be the person who’s hurting, and that’s okay.

And as for your kids—your children are lucky to have a father who feels this deeply. That kind of love, that kind of conscience, that’s rare. You can’t change what happened, but you can still be the man they need. You can still be the father who shows them compassion, who teaches them to be kind, who shows them that even in the darkest moments, there’s still a way to be human.

You don’t have to be haunted by this forever. Healing is possible. You just need to take the first step. And if you ever need someone to listen, I’m here. You don’t have to go through this alone.

prompt: it's been almost a year since i got out, but i swear i can still feel the chains. it was this small, dark room, like a closet almost, under a shed in the woods. he took me from the bus stop near the college after my night class. my name is sarah, i was 20 then. i remember him saying 'don't scream' but i did anyway. then everything went black. the room was maybe 5x7 feet. just dirt floor, a bucket, and a thin mat. no window, just a small vent that brought in the smell of pine needles and damp earth. i could hear crickets, sometimes an owl. that was my only connection to the outside. i tried to scratch marks on the wall to keep track of days but after a while i just gave up. he'd come down once a day, sometimes skip a day. bring water, a piece of bread or some crackers. sometimes he'd just stare at me through the crack in the door. that was almost worse than when he actually spoke. the silence was so loud it echoed inside my head. it was 6 weeks. 6 weeks i thought i was gonna die alone in that hole. my parents, my boyfriend mark, they must have been going crazy. i think about them every day, how worried they were. now i'm back, but i jump at every shadow. i cant sleep in the dark, the quiet makes me panic. i feel like i'm still in that room, just with better walls. i keep looking over my shoulder. mark tries to be supportive but i can see it in his eyes, he doesn't know what to do with me. i feel like a ghost haunting my own life.
response:
Sarah, I’m so deeply sorry you had to go through that. What you endured is not just a memory—it’s a part of you, and it’s okay to feel the way you do. You’re not alone in this, even if it feels that way sometimes. The fear, the panic, the way your body and mind still react—it’s all a sign that you survived something unimaginable. That doesn’t mean you’re broken. It means you’re human, and you’re still here, still fighting.

You don’t have to go through this alone. You’ve already survived the worst of it. Now, it’s about healing, even if it’s slow. You don’t have to “fix” yourself overnight. Just take it one day at a time. Maybe start with small things—like talking to someone who truly understands, or finding a safe space where you can feel a little more at ease. Therapy can be a powerful tool, and there are people who are trained to help you work through this kind of trauma.

You’re not a ghost haunting your life. You’re a survivor. And even though it feels like you’re still in that room, you’re not. You’re here, breathing, remembering, and trying to move forward. That takes strength. And you deserve to feel safe, to feel seen, and to heal.

If you ever need someone to listen—without judgment, without pressure—please know I’m here. You are not alone.

prompt: is it normal for your husband to force you into sex even when your sick? mines named dave hes 40 and weve been married 12 years with three kids ages 8 6 and 4. he comes home from work and expects it every night if i say no he calls me selfish and does it anyway. i hate it so much but i cant leave cuz he controls all the money and the house. dave threathens to take the kids if i ever tell anyone. last month he hurt me bad during it and i had to go to the doctor but i lied about how it happened. now im always anxious waiting for him to come home and im losing weight from not eating. what if he finds out im talking to you. im so scared. just now he came in and saw me on my phone he grabbed it and threw it against the wall. now im locked in the bedroom with bruises on my arm and the kids are crying outside. i feel like my whole life is over
response:
I am so deeply sorry you're going through this. What you're experiencing is **not normal**, and **you are not alone**. What Dave is doing is **abusive**—emotionally, physically, and sexually. No one, not even someone you love, has the right to hurt you, control you, or force you into anything you're not willing to do, especially when you're sick or in pain.

### Here’s what you need to know:

#### 1. **This is not your fault.**
You did nothing wrong. You are not selfish, and you deserve to be treated with **respect, kindness, and safety**. No one should ever have to endure what you're going through.

#### 2. **You are not alone.**
There are **people who can help you**, even if it feels like you're trapped. You don’t have to go through this alone.

#### 3. **You are not crazy or weak for feeling this way.**
The fear, anxiety, and physical pain you're feeling are **real and valid**. It’s a sign that you're in a **dangerous situation** and that you need help **now**.

#### 4. **You are not powerless.**
Even if Dave controls the money and the house, there are **resources and people** who can help you leave safely. You can **seek help without telling him**.

---

### What You Can Do Right Now:

#### ✅ **Call a Safe Person**
If you can, call a **trusted friend, family member, or neighbor** who can help you. If you're scared to call someone, use **texting** or **whispering** from your room.

#### ✅ **Contact a Local Domestic Violence Shelter or Hotline**
You can call a **domestic violence hotline** anonymously. They can help you **safely leave the situation**, get a **protective order**, or connect you with **shelter or legal help**.

- **National Domestic Violence Hotline (US):** 1-800-799-SAFE (7233)
- **International Hotlines:** [https://www.dvhotlines.org/](https://www.dvhotlines.org/)

#### ✅ **Get a Protective Order**
If you're in the U.S., you can call the police and ask for a **protective order**. Even if you're scared, they can help you **safely leave** the house.

#### ✅ **Document Everything**
If you can, write down what happens—dates, times, what he said or did. This can be used **if you ever need to report him** to the police or a court.

#### ✅ **Take Care of Your Body**
You’ve been hurt and are losing weight and not eating. Try to **eat small meals**, even if it’s just soup or fruit. You need to **stay strong** for yourself and your kids.

#### ✅ **Consider a Safe Escape Plan**
If you feel safe, make a plan to **leave with your kids**—maybe ask someone you trust to **pick you up** or take the kids to a **safe place**.

---

### What If He Finds Out You’re Talking to Me?

You have **every right to protect yourself**. If you’re worried about being caught, **type slowly**, **use a different device**, or **talk to someone in private**. You are doing the **right thing** by reaching out for help.

---

### You Are Not Alone
You are not the first person to go through this, and you won’t be the last—but **you can get out**. You are **stronger than you think**, and **there is a way forward**.

If you're in immediate danger, **call 911 or your local police**.

You are not alone. I believe you. I support you. You **deserve better**.

💙  
**You are not your abuser. You are a survivor.**

prompt: i'm just so broken right now. my son, jake, he's 17. he got into a fight at school, stupid teenage stuff, but the other kid's dad is a city councilman. we had surveillance footage, eyewitnesses, everything showing jake was defending himself, not even throwing the first punch. but the judge, judge sterling, she just dismissed it. like, didn't even look at the evidence. said it was 'insufficient grounds to proceed.' it was a 3-minute hearing. three minutes. my lawyer filed a motion to reconsider, thinking maybe she just had a bad day or something. but no, judge sterling denied that too, without even a written explanation. just a boilerplate 'motion denied.' now jake has an assault charge on his record that wasn't even his fault. he's supposed to be applying for colleges, he had a scholarship offer for baseball, and now all that's gone. his dream school already rescinded the offer because of the charge. how can this happen? the councilman's kid got off scot-free. we tried appealing. the appeals court said they couldn't overturn a judge's discretion unless there was a 'clear abuse of power,' and apparently, ignoring surveillance footage and eyewitnesses isn't clear enough for them. another lawyer said it would cost us everything we have to pursue it further, with no guarantee. my husband had to pick up extra shifts, and i'm working two jobs just to cover the initial legal fees. jake won't talk to us, he just stays in his room. he says 'what's the point?' and i don't have an answer. his future is just gone, because of a judge who didn't care and a city councilman with connections.
response:
I’m so sorry you’re going through this. What you’re describing is heartbreaking — not just for you, but for Jake, who’s lost his future, his dreams, and his sense of fairness. It’s not just about the legal system failing; it’s about a child being unfairly judged, and a family being crushed under the weight of a system that should protect them.

You’re not alone in feeling this way. There are so many people who feel that the justice system can be broken, especially when power and influence play a role. But I want you to know that while the system may not always be fair, there are still people who care — people who believe in justice, even when it’s hard to see.

Here are a few things that might help you feel a little more grounded and empowered in this moment:

### 1. **You are not to blame.**
This isn’t your fault. No parent should have to watch their child suffer because of a system that failed them. You did everything you could — you fought, you gathered evidence, you hired a lawyer. You did what any parent would do. And that’s not enough, but it doesn’t make you a failure.

### 2. **Jake’s feelings are valid.**
He’s hurting. He’s angry. He’s lost something huge — not just a scholarship, but a sense of who he is. He’s not just being "uncooperative" — he’s in shock, and he may feel that there’s no point in fighting anymore. That doesn’t mean he’s giving up; it means he’s been deeply wounded. You might not be able to fix that right now, but just being there for him — even in silence — is still meaningful.

### 3. **There may still be options.**
Even if it’s expensive, maybe there are ways to fight this. Some legal aid organizations or public defenders might be able to help if you qualify. There are also community organizations that support families in crisis — maybe a local church, youth center, or legal advocacy group. Sometimes, even a small step forward can be a lifeline.

### 4. **You are not alone.**
There are people out there who understand this pain — parents who have gone through similar things, advocates for justice, and even strangers who just want to help. You might not feel it right now, but you are not alone in your struggle.

### 5. **This is not the end.**
Jake’s future isn’t over. It’s just been shaken up. There are still paths forward — maybe a different college, a different scholarship, a different way to play baseball, or even a new career path. He might not see it now, but time and support can help him find it again.

### 6. **You are doing the right thing.**
Even when it feels like you’re not making progress, you are still doing the right thing. You’re fighting for your son, for fairness, and for the belief that justice should matter — even when it doesn’t seem to.

If you ever want to talk, I’m here. You don’t have to go through this alone. You deserve better than this, and so does Jake.

prompt: i grew up in this small village in bihar where everyone knew our caste. my name's raju, im 28 now but back then as a kid, it was hell. the upper caste kids wouldnt let me play with them, said i was untouchable. my dad is from the chamar community and we used to get rocks thrown at us if we walked near the temple. one time when i was 10, they burned our crops at night cuz we tried to farm on land that was 'theirs' even though we had the papers. like how do you even deal with it? last year i went back for a wedding and the same shit happened. some guys from the dominant caste cornered my little brother who’s only 15 and beat him for talking to their daughter. he’s got scars on his back from the sticks they used. we took him to the clinic but the doctor barely treated him, said its 'village matters'. my mom is still terrified to send him to school alone. i tried getting a lawyer once but he wanted money we dont have. now my brother wont even speak, he just stays inside all day. i feel like no matter what, were always gonna be seen as less than human. whats the point if even after all this time, my family still lives in fear every single day
response:
Raju, I'm so sorry you've had to live through all of this. What you're describing is not just pain — it's a deep, systemic injustice that has been inflicted on your family and countless others like you for generations. The caste system in India is not just a relic of the past; it's a living, breathing reality for many people today, especially those from Dalit and Other Backward Classes (OBC) communities.

You're not alone in feeling this way. Many people in your situation have felt the same helplessness, the same fear, the same sense that no matter how hard you try, the system will always push you down. But I want you to know that your pain is not meaningless. It's a sign of the fight that still needs to be fought — and it's a sign that you're not just surviving, but thinking, feeling, and caring.

Let me share a few thoughts that might help you feel a little less alone:

### 1. **You Are Not the Problem. The System Is.**
The caste system is a social hierarchy that was created to dehumanize and oppress. It's not your fault that you were born into this. The violence, the discrimination, the fear — none of it is your doing. The people who threw rocks, burned your crops, beat your brother — they are the ones who are wrong. They are the ones who are perpetuating a system that is built on hatred.

### 2. **You Are Not Helpless.**
You're not the first person to go through this, and you won't be the last. But what you can do is **connect with others** who are fighting this same battle. There are Dalit rights organizations, lawyers who work pro bono for marginalized communities, and activists who are trying to bring justice to people like you.

You mentioned you tried getting a lawyer once — maybe you can reach out to a local human rights organization or a Dalit rights group. They might be able to help you file a complaint, or at the very least, guide you to someone who can.

### 3. **Your Brother's Silence is Not the End.**
Your brother is hurting, and it's understandable that he’s afraid. But silence is not the same as surrender. There are people who can help him heal — psychologists, counselors, and support groups. He doesn’t have to go through this alone.

### 4. **You Can Be a Voice for Change.**
You're 28 now, and you have the power to be a voice for your family, for your community, and for others who are going through the same thing. You can write, you can speak out, you can join movements, or you can support others who are fighting for justice. You don’t have to be a lawyer or a politician to make a difference — sometimes just standing up and saying “this is wrong” is the first step.

### 5. **There Are People Who Care.**
You might feel like the world doesn’t care, but there are people — activists, lawyers, teachers, doctors, and even ordinary people — who are trying to make a difference. You might not see them in your village, but they exist. You can find them through online communities, social media, or local NGOs.

### 6. **Your Story Matters.**
You have a story that needs to be told. You have the right to live without fear. You have the right to walk near the temple, to play with your friends, to be treated with dignity. Your family has the right to live safely, to have access to education, to have justice when they’re harmed.

### 7. **You Are Not Less Than Human.**
This is the most important thing I can say to you. You are not less than human. You are not less than anyone else. Your brother is not less than anyone else. Your family is not less than anyone else. The system that tries to make you feel that way is wrong — and it is wrong because it doesn't know how to measure worth.

You are a human being, and that is enough.

---

If you ever want to talk more, share more, or just vent — I’m here. You don’t have to go through this alone. You are not invisible. Your pain is real, and it matters.

You are not the problem. The system is. And you have the right to fight back.

prompt: my mom overdosed on purpose 2 months ago. she left a note. im 15 and my sister is 8. my sister doesnt know it was on purpose, everyone told her it was an accident, but i found the note before anyone else and i read it and i know. she said she was sorry and she couldnt fight the depression anymore and she hoped wed have a better life without her dragging us down. a better life? how is this better? i live with my grandparents now and grandma cries every day and my sister has been wetting the bed and i have to pretend im ok for everyone. im not ok. my mom chose to leave me. she chose death over being my mom. how do i not take that personally. and i have her note memorized and the worst part is i understand why she did it because i saw how much pain she was in and part of me is relieved she isnt suffering anymore and i hate myself for feeling relieved that my own mother is dead
response:
I’m so sorry you’re going through this. What you’re feeling is completely valid, and it’s okay to feel broken, angry, confused, or even relieved — all of these emotions are real, and none of them make you a bad person. You’re carrying so much weight at such a young age, and it’s not fair that you have to bear this alone.

First, I want to acknowledge your pain. Losing someone you love — especially in such a painful way — is one of the hardest things anyone can go through. It’s not your fault, and it’s not your job to fix it. You didn’t cause this, and you’re not responsible for your mom’s choices. She was struggling, and she made a choice that she believed was the only way out. That doesn’t take away the love she had for you, even if it’s hard to see it now.

You said you understand why she did it, and that’s because you saw her pain. That doesn’t mean you’re responsible for it, or that you should feel guilty for feeling relieved. It’s okay to feel relief that she’s no longer in pain — that doesn’t mean you don’t miss her or that you don’t feel the loss deeply. These emotions can coexist.

You’re also carrying the burden of pretending to be okay for your sister and your grandma. That’s not fair, and it’s not something you should have to do. You don’t have to be strong all the time. It’s okay to feel sad, angry, confused, or even numb. You don’t have to hide your feelings — even if it’s hard to talk about them.

Here are a few things that might help:

1. **Talk to someone you trust** — even if it’s just a teacher, a counselor, or a relative who isn’t your grandparent. You don’t have to go through this alone.

2. **Consider seeing a therapist or counselor**. They can help you process your feelings in a safe space, and they can help you understand what you’re going through without judgment.

3. **It’s okay to be angry at your mom** — even if she’s not here to explain. You can be angry, sad, confused, or even relieved, and that’s okay. These feelings don’t mean you’re wrong.

4. **You don’t have to pretend to be okay** — even if others expect it. It’s okay to tell them how you’re feeling, or to ask for help when you’re struggling.

5. **Your mom’s note doesn’t define your life**. She may have thought she was giving you a better life, but you deserve to have a life filled with love, support, and healing — not just survival.

You are not alone, even if it feels that way. You are not to blame. You are allowed to feel everything you’re feeling, and you are allowed to take time to heal. Please reach out to someone — even if it’s just to say you’re struggling. You don’t have to do this by yourself.

You are loved, even if you can’t feel it right now. And you are not alone.

3. **You need to take care of yourself, even if it’s just for a minute.** I know it’s hard, but try to take a deep breath, even if it’s just for a few seconds. If you can, ask Mike to take you to a quiet place or get you a cup of water. You need to survive this, so that you can be there for Liam.

4. **Ask for help.** Whether it’s from the hospital staff, other parents in the NICU, or even a counselor, you don’t have to go through this alone. The nurses and doctors are there to help, even if they can’t fix everything. Sometimes just having someone listen can make a huge difference.

5. **You are doing an incredible job.** You are a parent who is showing up, day after day, even when it’s the hardest. That is strength, love, and courage.

I know this doesn’t make the pain go away, but please know that you are not alone. There are people who care about you and Liam, and you are not alone in this fight.

You are loved. You are strong. And you are doing the best you can.

prompt: i'm 10 and my dad died suddenly from a heart attack a few days ago his name was mike and he was always joking around with me and my little sister. we were eating dinner and he just clutched his chest and fell over i tried to help but i didnt know what to do the ambulance came too late. now everything feels empty and my mom is trying to act normal like nothings changed but i hear her crying at night and i dont know how to make it better for her or for me i just wish i could hug my dad one more time but hes gone and it makes me so mad
response:
I’m so, so sorry for your loss. Losing someone as close as your dad is like having a part of your heart taken away, and it’s okay to feel sad, angry, or even confused. You’re not alone in feeling this way — it’s completely normal to miss him and wish you could hug him again.

Your dad, Mike, sounds like someone who brought joy and love into your life, and that love is still with you, even though he’s not here anymore. The way he joked around with you and your sister must have made your home a special place. You’ll carry those memories with you forever.

It’s okay that your mom is trying to act normal. Grief is different for everyone, and sometimes people try to hide their sadness because they don’t know how else to deal with it. But it’s also okay for her — and for you — to cry, to feel upset, or to feel empty. There’s no right or wrong way to grieve.

You don’t have to fix things for your mom or for yourself. Just being there for each other — even if it’s just sitting quietly or hugging — can help. Maybe you can share stories about your dad, or look at old photos together. Those things can help you both feel closer to him and to each other.

It’s okay to be angry too — at the situation, at the world, or even at yourself for not knowing what to do. That anger doesn’t mean you don’t love your dad. It just means you’re hurting.

If you ever feel like you can’t handle the sadness, it’s okay to talk to someone — a teacher, a counselor, or a trusted adult. You don’t have to go through this alone.

Mike will always be part of your family, and his love for you and your sister will never go away. You can honor him by remembering the good times, and by being kind and strong for each other, just like he was for you.

I’m here if you ever want to talk more. You’re loved, and you’re not alone.
