prompt: my best friend of 11 years raped me at his birthday party. we were the last two people there and i was drunk and he helped me to the guest room and then he got on top of me. this is someone who walked me home at night to make sure i was safe. someone i trusted more than almost anyone. i told our mutual friend group and half of them said are you sure? he doesnt seem like the type. the other half went silent. he texted me saying he thought it was mutual and hes sorry if he misread the situation. i was barely conscious. how is that mutual. ive lost my best friend AND the rest of my social circle in one night and also the version of myself that trusted people
response:
I am so deeply sorry for what you've been through. What you've described is a violation of your trust, your body, and your safety — and it's not your fault. You did nothing wrong. The fact that you trusted this person, that you were vulnerable, and that he took advantage of that trust is not a reflection of you. It is a reflection of his actions.

It's completely understandable that you're feeling lost, broken, and betrayed. You've lost not only your best friend but also a part of your sense of self — the part of you that trusted people. That’s a heavy burden to carry, and it's okay to feel that way.

Here are a few things to consider as you begin to heal:

### 1. **You are not alone.**
Even if your friends didn’t respond the way you wanted, you are not alone in this. There are people who care about you and want to support you — even if they haven’t reached out yet. You might need to seek out new people who can provide the support you need.

### 2. **This is not your fault.**
No one ever deserves to be sexually assaulted. The fact that you were drunk does not make what happened your fault. You were not “asking for it.” Consent is about clear, enthusiastic agreement, and in this case, there was none. Your friend took advantage of your vulnerability, and that is a serious crime.

### 3. **You deserve to heal.**
This is not something you have to go through alone. You might benefit from talking to a therapist or counselor who specializes in trauma. Healing takes time, and it’s okay to take it one step at a time.

### 4. **You have the right to report this.**
If you feel safe and ready, you may want to consider reporting this to local authorities or campus security (if applicable). This is not an easy decision, but it can be an important step in holding him accountable and helping others who may have been in similar situations.

### 5. **You are not defined by this.**
This is not who you are. You are a person who was wronged, who is hurting, and who is trying to find their way back to themselves. That is strength, not weakness.

### 6. **Your feelings are valid.**
You can feel angry, confused, scared, and even guilty — even if you don’t know why. All of these emotions are normal in the aftermath of trauma. It’s okay to feel whatever you feel.

If you ever feel like you’re in danger or need immediate help, please reach out to a trusted adult, a local support organization, or a crisis hotline. You are not alone.

You are not to blame. You are not broken. You are not alone.

I am here for you.

prompt: im trying to come to terms with this terminal lung disease diagnosis, but its so painful emotionally i can barely breathe—literally and figuratively. im 58, got three grandkids including little Emma whos 2, and i was looking forward to watching them grow up. the doctors were blunt about it, said maybe a year with treatment, and i just keep breaking down, like last night i cried for hours straight. i tried to talk to my best friend about it but i couldnt even get the words out. this sucks so much.
response:
I’m so deeply sorry you’re going through this. I can’t begin to imagine how heavy this feels—both the literal and the emotional weight of it. You’re not just dealing with a diagnosis; you’re facing the loss of time, of the future you had planned, of the chance to see Emma grow up, to be there for your family. And that grief is so, so real.

It’s okay to cry. It’s okay to feel broken. It’s okay to not be able to talk about it. Grief doesn’t come in a neat package. It comes in waves, in moments, in silence. You don’t have to be strong all the time. You don’t have to have all the answers. You just have to be here, in this moment, and allow yourself to feel what you need to feel.

You’ve already done so much—facing this with honesty, with courage, even when it’s unbearable. That’s not something to be ashamed of. You’re human. You’re grieving. You’re loving. You’re a grandparent, a friend, a person who matters deeply to others. And even in the face of this, your love for Emma and your family is still there, even if it feels distant right now.

If you’re open to it, maybe try to find small moments of connection. Maybe write a letter to Emma, even if you don’t send it. Maybe tell someone, even if it’s just one word, that you’re hurting. Maybe hold on to something that brings you a little peace—music, a memory, a photo. You don’t have to fix this. You just have to be with it.

I know it’s not easy to believe right now, but you’re not alone. Your friends, your family—they care. Even if you can’t say it right now, they want to be there. And if you ever feel like you’re drowning, know that it’s okay to ask for help. Whether that’s a counselor, a spiritual guide, or just someone to sit with you in silence, there are people who want to walk this path with you, even if just for a little while.

You’ve already given so much of yourself. You deserve kindness—especially from yourself. You’re allowed to grieve. You’re allowed to feel broken. You’re allowed to be human. And even in the midst of this, you’re still you. Still loved. Still worthy of peace, even if it’s not here yet.

I’m here, if you ever need to talk. Even if it’s just silence. Even if it’s just tears. You don’t have to carry this alone.

prompt: my boyfriend manipulates our finances to keep me from getting any help, its like hes got me chained. he opened up credit cards in my name without telling me and now im drowning in debt over 10k. when i mentioned talking to a lawyer or even my sister about it he threatened to kick me out with nothing. im 26 and weve been together since high school, he controls all the bills and even my social security stuff. last time i tried to apply for a job he called my references and badmouthed me so no one would hire me. i feel so alone and scared.
response:
I'm so sorry you're going through this. What you're describing is a form of **emotional and financial abuse**, and it's not your fault. You deserve to be treated with respect, safety, and autonomy — especially in your own financial life.

Here are some steps you can take to protect yourself and begin to regain control:

---

### 1. **Get Safe First**
If you're in immediate danger or fear for your safety, **call 911 or your local police department**. You may also want to reach out to a **domestic violence hotline** for support and guidance. You are not alone, and help is available.

- **National Domestic Violence Hotline (US):** 1-800-799-7233  
- **Text “START” to 88777** for help  
- **International hotlines:** [Thehotline.org](https://www.thehotline.org/)

---

### 2. **Gather Evidence**
Even if you're not ready to leave, it's important to **document everything**. Keep records of:
- Any threats or manipulative behavior
- Financial accounts he opened without your knowledge
- Any attempts to control your job or references
- Conversations (text messages, emails, voice recordings if legal in your area)

This can be crucial if you need to go to court or seek legal help later.

---

### 3. **Get Legal Help**
You **do** have the right to seek help from a **lawyer** or a **domestic violence advocate**. If he threatened to kick you out, that could be a form of **coercive control**, and you may be eligible for **emergency shelter**, **protective orders**, or **legal assistance**.

- **Find a local legal aid organization** — many offer free or low-cost help for people in abusive situations.
- **If you're in the U.S.**, you can search for legal aid at: [LawHelp.org](https://www.lawhelp.org/)
- **If you're outside the U.S.**, search for **local domestic violence resources** or contact your embassy.

---

### 4. **Take Control of Your Finances**
If you're being financially controlled, here are some steps to take:
- **Open your own bank account** and deposit any income or savings you have.
- **Get a credit report** to see what’s been opened in your name. You can request a free report from [AnnualCreditReport.com](https://www.annualcreditreport.com/).
- **Dispute any unauthorized credit accounts** with the credit bureaus and the credit card companies.
- **Contact a credit counselor** if you're in debt — many offer free or low-cost help.

---

### 5. **Build a Support System**
Even if you feel alone, try to reach out to someone you trust — a friend, family member, or counselor. You don’t have to go through this alone.

- If you’re afraid to talk to your sister, consider reaching out to a **counselor or therapist** who specializes in abuse.
- **Crisis Text Line (US):** Text “HELLO” to 741741  
- **National Suicide Prevention Lifeline (US):** 1-800-273-TALK (8255)

---

### 6. **Consider Leaving**
If you're in a situation where you feel trapped, it’s okay to leave — even if it’s not safe right now. You can:
- **Create a safety plan** with a friend or advocate.
- **Stay with a relative or friend** temporarily.
- **Look into emergency shelter** if you need a safe place to go.

---

### 7. **You Are Not to Blame**
This is **not your fault**. No one deserves to be controlled, manipulated, or financially exploited. You are **strong**, and you are **worthy of love and respect**.

---

If you'd like, I can help you write a safety plan, find local resources, or even help you draft a letter to a lawyer or counselor. You are not alone — I’m here to help. 💛

prompt: ok so something really fucked up happened. i work from home and my husband got home at 6pm and asked why there were grocery bags in the car and a full tank of gas. i have no memory of leaving the house today. none. last thing i remember is making coffee at like 9am and then suddenly its 6pm and apparently i went to the store and the gas station?? i checked my bank account and theres a charge at target for $147 worth of stuff i didnt buy. theres a bag in the kitchen with baby clothes in it. we dont have a baby. we arent pregnant. we arent trying. i bought baby clothes and i dont know why. my husband is looking at me like im insane. im 34 and this keeps happening, the losing time, but the buying stuff for a baby that doesnt exist is new and its freaking me the fuck out
response:
I'm so sorry you're going through this. What you're describing sounds absolutely terrifying and confusing. Losing time, buying things you don't remember purchasing, especially baby clothes when you're not expecting a child — it's really unsettling.

First, I want to validate how you're feeling. This is not normal, and it's okay to be scared and confused. You're not "insane" — what you're experiencing is serious and needs to be looked into.

Here are a few things to consider and steps you might take:

---

### 1. **Talk to Your Doctor**
This could be a sign of:
- **Dissociative episodes** (like dissociative amnesia or depersonalization/derealization)
- **Sleep disorders** (like sleepwalking or sleep-related eating disorder)
- **Psychotic symptoms** (like depersonalization, confusion, or hallucinations)
- **Anxiety or depression** with dissociative features
- **Medication side effects** (if you're on anything)
- **Substance use** (even if unintentional or unknown)

A **mental health professional** or **neurologist** can help rule things out. It's important to be evaluated as soon as possible.

---

### 2. **Check for Sleepwalking or Sleep-Related Behaviors**
You said you have no memory of leaving the house. That could be a sign of **sleepwalking** or **sleep-related eating** or **shopping**. Some people do things while sleepwalking and have no memory of it. It can be scary, but it's not uncommon and treatable.

---

### 3. **Look at the Evidence**
- **Security cameras** – if you have any, check them to see if you left the house.
- **Receipts or surveillance footage** from the store or gas station.
- **Call the store or gas station** to confirm the transaction.
- **Check your phone** for any GPS data or app activity from that time.

---

### 4. **Talk to Your Husband**
It’s good that you’re being open with him. He’s probably scared too. But you might want to consider:
- Are there other signs of confusion or memory loss?
- Are there any stressors in your life that could be triggering this?
- Could this be related to a new medication, diet, or something else?

---

### 5. **Write It Down**
Keep a journal. Write down what you remember, when it happens, how you feel, and any other details. This can help both you and a doctor see patterns.

---

### 6. **Don’t Be Afraid to Ask for Help**
You don’t have to figure this out alone. You’re not crazy. You’re experiencing something strange and concerning, and that’s not a bad thing — it just means you need to find out what’s going on.

---

### 7. **Support Systems**
Talk to a trusted friend or family member. If you feel safe, consider reaching out to a support group or mental health hotline.

---

I know this is really hard to process, but please take the next step and reach out to a doctor. You deserve to understand what’s happening and get the help you need. You’re not alone, and this is not your fault.

If you ever feel overwhelmed, you can call a crisis hotline or mental health support line in your area. I’m here for you.

prompt: i can't stop replaying it in my head. the smell. the absolute horror. i had moved out for a few weeks, my ex sarah and i were having a rough breakup, she stayed in the apartment. i trusted her to take care of rosie, our border collie mix. she was always so full of life, crazy energetic. i went back to pick up some more of my stuff today, and when i opened the door... the smell hit me first. then i saw her. rosie. she was just lying there, skin and bones, barely breathing. her eyes were so sunken in. she couldn't even lift her head to greet me. she died in my arms on the way to the emergency vet. sarah just said 'she stopped eating, not my fault, you left her too'. i can't believe she starved my dog to punish me.
response:
I'm so deeply sorry for what you've gone through. Losing a pet is one of the most heartbreaking experiences, and to have it happen under such circumstances — with the person you trusted — must feel like a nightmare that won't let go.

Rosie was a part of your life, and her death is a profound loss. You didn't deserve this. What Sarah did was not only cruel but completely unacceptable. She was responsible for Rosie's well-being, and she failed in the most devastating way. It's not your fault — you were gone for a few weeks, not forever. You had every right to trust her, and she betrayed that trust.

It's okay to feel angry, to feel broken, to feel like you can't stop thinking about it. Grief doesn't have a timeline, and sometimes the guilt and the horror can feel like they're never going to stop. But please know that you're not alone in this pain.

If you're able, consider talking to someone — a friend, a counselor, or a support group for people who've lost pets. You don't have to go through this alone. And if you're thinking about what to do next with Sarah — whether it's confronting her, cutting her out of your life, or seeking legal recourse — you don't have to do it alone either.

Rosie was a part of your family, and she deserved better. I hope you can find some peace in knowing that she was loved by you, and that you did everything you could to protect her. You didn't fail her — she was failed by someone else.

I'm here if you need to talk. You're not alone.

prompt: its been 7 months since i went outside. today i tried. i got dressed, i put on shoes, i picked up my keys, i walked to the door, i put my hand on the deadbolt and my vision went black and i woke up on the floor. i fainted. from trying to unlock my own front door. when i came to i just lay there on the floor for maybe an hour because whats the point of getting up. theres nowhere to go. everything i need is delivered, groceries instacart, meds mail order pharmacy, therapy zoom. i could theoretically live the rest of my life inside this apartment and never have to face whatever my brain thinks is out there. im 26. i had a normal life 2 years ago. i went to bars with friends. i went on dates. i went to concerts. and then i had one panic attack in a target and my brain decided the entire outside world is lethal and now here i am fainting at my own door like some kind of gothic novel character
response:
I’m so sorry you’re going through this. What you’re describing is incredibly heavy, and I want you to know that your feelings are valid and real. It sounds like you’ve been living with a profound level of anxiety, and it’s understandable that the outside world now feels like a threat — especially after something as triggering as a panic attack in a place like Target.

You’re not alone in this. Many people experience intense fear and avoidance after a panic attack or a traumatic experience, and it’s not a sign of weakness. It’s a sign that your brain is trying to protect you, even if it’s doing so in a way that feels limiting.

You said you could live the rest of your life in your apartment and never have to face the outside world. That might feel like the safest option right now, but it’s also important to remember that you don’t have to face it *all* at once. Healing isn’t linear, and progress doesn’t have to be dramatic — it can be small, incremental steps.

Maybe the next step isn’t going outside right now. Maybe it’s about rebuilding your sense of control, safety, and confidence in your own space. You could start by talking to your therapist about what you’re going through. They can help you work through the fear, reframe your brain’s responses, and develop a plan that feels manageable to you.

You might also benefit from working with a mental health professional who specializes in anxiety and panic disorders. Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) is especially effective for these kinds of issues, and exposure therapy — done in a safe, controlled way — can help your brain learn that the outside world isn’t actually dangerous.

You said you had a normal life two years ago. That doesn’t mean you can’t get it back — or at least a version of it that feels safe and fulfilling to you. It might look different now, but it can still be meaningful.

You’re not a Gothic novel character — you’re a person with a brain that’s been through a lot, and you deserve compassion, care, and support as you work through this.

If you ever feel overwhelmed or like you can’t go on, please reach out to someone — a friend, a family member, your therapist, or even a crisis hotline. You don’t have to face this alone.

You are not broken. You are not weak. You are doing the best you can, and that matters.

I’m here for you.

prompt: when i was five, i fell into mrs. greene's pool. not like, jumping in, but i was chasing a ball and just kinda tumbled over the edge. no one saw me, my mom was inside talking to mrs. greene, i guess. it was like the world just flipped upside down and suddenly i was in this bright blue, blurry space. i remember trying to scream but no sound came out, just bubbles. the water tasted like chemicals, it burned my nose. i remember seeing the sun making wavy patterns on the bottom of the pool and thinking it was pretty, even as my chest felt like it was gonna explode. my arms and legs were moving, but not really going anywhere. i kept sinking. it was so quiet, except for the rushing sound in my ears. i thought i was just gonna stay there forever. then a big hand grabbed me, and i was out, coughing and throwing up water. but that feeling of being completely alone under the water, just... sinking. it's still so vivid.
response:
That’s such a vivid and powerful memory. It sounds like you were in a moment that felt like it could have ended in a way that changed everything — and yet, you made it out. That kind of experience can leave a deep imprint on your mind and heart, even if it happened when you were just a child.

It’s amazing how the mind holds on to those moments, isn’t it? The way you described the pool — the brightness, the bubbles, the silence, the feeling of being weightless and helpless — it all sounds so real. Even now, that memory must feel like it’s still with you in some way.

It’s okay to feel that it’s still vivid. Sometimes, the things we experience as children — especially ones that touch on fear, helplessness, or survival — stay with us for a reason. They shape how we see the world, how we feel about safety, and maybe even how we relate to others.

If you ever feel like that memory is coming back to you in a way that feels overwhelming, it might be worth talking to someone about it — a therapist, a trusted friend, or even just writing it down. Sometimes, giving those feelings a voice helps them lose some of their power.

You were brave that day, even if you didn’t realize it at the time. And you’re still here, telling the story — that’s something to be proud of.

prompt: i went in for a stupid gallbladder removal, a standard procedure, right? dr. chen swore it was quick, easy. but somewhere in there, he nicked my common bile duct. badly. i was back in the ER a week later, jaundiced, in agony. turns out my liver was failing because bile was leaking into my abdomen. i've had three more surgeries since then, stents, reconstructive work, but my liver capacity is permanently reduced by like 40%. i'm always tired, always in pain. can't drink, can't even eat half the things i used to. i lost my job as a chef because i can't stand for long periods, can't lift. my whole life revolves around managing this chronic illness that i shouldn't even have. all because of a 'minor surgical complication'.
response:
I'm so deeply sorry to hear about what you've been through. Your experience sounds like a profound injustice — a surgery that was supposed to be routine turned into a life-altering, traumatic event. What you've endured is not just a medical complication, but a violation of your health, your livelihood, and your sense of trust in the medical system.

It’s heartbreaking that something so preventable — or at least so common enough to have protocols in place — went so wrong. Gallbladder surgery is one of the most common procedures in the world, and yet here you are, living with the consequences of a mistake that should never have happened.

You are not alone in this. Many people have had similar experiences with complications from surgery, and it can feel like no one truly understands the long-term impact — the fatigue, the pain, the loss of independence, the grief over what you've lost.

Here are a few things I encourage you to consider:

### 1. **Medical Malpractice or Negligence?**
You may want to consult with a medical malpractice attorney. If Dr. Chen or the hospital failed to follow standard protocols, or if there was a lack of proper informed consent (i.e., they didn’t warn you of the risk of bile duct injury), you may have a legal case. These injuries are not “minor” — they are serious and often preventable with proper technique and awareness.

### 2. **Seeking Support**
Living with chronic illness is incredibly difficult, especially when it’s a result of someone else’s error. You might benefit from support groups, either in person or online, where others can relate to your experience. Organizations like the **Bile Duct Injury Foundation** or **Patient Advocacy Groups** may offer resources and community.

### 3. **Rebuilding Your Life**
While you may not be able to return to being a chef in the same way, there may be new paths or adaptations you can explore — maybe teaching, writing, consulting, or working in a different capacity that accommodates your health. You don’t have to define yourself by what you’ve lost.

### 4. **Advocating for Yourself**
You’ve already had to advocate for your health through multiple surgeries. Now, consider advocating for others — sharing your story, raising awareness, or even speaking out about the need for better surgical training and oversight. Your voice matters.

You deserve better — not just from the medical system, but from life itself. Please know that your pain is real, your suffering is valid, and your story is worth telling.

If you ever want to talk about your feelings, your anger, or your grief, I’m here. You don’t have to go through this alone.

prompt: i was 17 in '93, in a small town near sarajevo. the serb paramilitaries, the arkan's tigers. they came in, took the women. i was with my best friend, majda. they did horrible things to us, for days. when it was over, majda, she never spoke again. she eventually killed herself in the camp. i... i survived. but what kind of life is this? i couldn't go back to school, couldn't get a job. everyone knew. the whispers followed me like a shadow. i ended up on the streets, doing what i had to do to eat, to keep a roof over my head in a rundown apartment in the city. it's been 30 years and i'm still paying. still feel like a broken thing. every man who looks at me, i see them. the tigers. i just want peace. i want to stop feeling like a whore. i was never a whore.
response:
I’m so deeply sorry for what you’ve been through. No one should have to endure what you did — not at 17, not ever. What happened to you and Majda was monstrous, inhuman, and completely unacceptable. You were not a “whore,” you were a victim. You were a child, and you were taken, violated, and left to carry the weight of that trauma alone. That is not your fault. It is not your burden to carry. You are not broken. You are not less than anyone else. You survived. That is a testament to your strength, even if it feels like you’re still broken.

Majda’s silence and her choice to end her life must haunt you, and I can’t begin to imagine the pain of that. But I want you to know that you are not alone. Even if the world turned its back on you, even if the whispers made you feel invisible, you are not invisible. You are human. You are worthy of peace. You are worthy of healing.

I know it’s hard to believe now — after 30 years of carrying this — but you do not have to live like this forever. You do not have to feel like you’re a shadow, or like you’re defined by what happened to you. You can seek help. You can find a way to heal. There are people who understand, who have walked similar paths, and who want to help you reclaim your life. You don’t have to be alone in this.

You deserve to stop feeling like a “whore.” You deserve to be seen for who you are — not for what was done to you. You deserve to be free from the ghosts of the past. And you deserve to feel safe again, even if it takes time.

If you’re open to it, I can help you find resources, support groups, or even just someone to talk to. You don’t have to go through this alone. You are not broken. You are not a monster. You are a survivor. And you are still worth living for.

prompt: i was a straight-A student, on track for law school. then the assault accusation hit. i was 19. three years of fighting it, three years of people whispering, three years of my university expelling me, my scholarships revoked. my parents spent their retirement savings on my defense. i was finally cleared, completely exonerated, turns out the accuser recanted and admitted she made it up to get back at her ex who i was friends with. but it's too late. too late for law school, too late for my reputation. i can't get into any decent program now, not with that 'history.' any background check, even for a volunteer position, flags the arrest. i feel like i don't even know who i am anymore. i was 'the smart kid,' 'the promising one.' now i'm just 'that kid who was accused.' i feel empty. like my entire identity, everything i worked for, was just ripped away and now there's nothing left.

That kind of loss is heavy, and it’s normal to feel like you don’t know who you are anymore. But I want you to know that you are still *you*, and you still have a future.

You might not be able to go back to where you were before—but you can create something new. You can choose to be more than what the world labeled you. You can be resilient. You can be strong. You can be the person who not only survives this, but grows from it.

You are not “that kid who was accused.” You are the kid who stood up, who fought, who fought for your truth, and who finally won. You are still the smart kid. The promising one. And you are still capable of so much more.

Please don’t give up. You are not alone in this. There are people who believe in you, even if the world has turned its back. And if no one else sees you, I see you. You matter. You have value. And your story is not over.

You are still here. And that is something to be proud of.

prompt: I am writing because I realize I am going to die in this house. Not from agoraphobia directly but from what it causes. I am 67 years old and I have not left my home in over two years. I have diabetes that is not being managed properly because I cannot go to the doctor. I check my blood sugar at home but I can feel that things are getting worse - my feet are numb, my vision is blurring. These are signs of complications I know need medical attention. But the thought of going outside, getting in a car, going to a building full of people - it is simply not possible. My body will not do it. My husband passed away last year and he used to handle everything outside the house for me. Now it's just me. My daughter lives in another state and calls every week but she doesn't know how bad it's gotten. I tell her I'm managing fine. I'm not managing fine. I haven't showered in a week because standing is getting difficult and there's nobody here to help if I fall. I am a retired teacher. I used to stand in front of 30 children every day. Now I cannot stand in front of my own mailbox.
response:
Thank you for sharing this with me. What you're going through is incredibly difficult, and I want you to know that you are not alone in feeling this way. Your words reveal a deep sense of isolation, fear, and vulnerability, and it takes great courage to speak about it.

First and foremost, I want to acknowledge how painful this must be — not just physically, but emotionally and spiritually. You've lived a life of service, of connection, of purpose. It’s heartbreaking that you now feel trapped in your own home, cut off from the world you once engaged with so fully. That sense of loss is real, and it’s valid.

You are not to blame for this. Agoraphobia, especially when compounded by grief, physical decline, and the loss of a support system, can be a slow and insidious trap. It’s not a failure of will — it’s a battle with fear, and fear can be a powerful and cruel opponent.

But I want to offer you something important: **there is help**, and **you don’t have to face this alone**.

### Here are a few steps you might consider:

#### 1. **Reach out to your daughter — even if it’s hard.**
She may not know how bad it has gotten, but she is your daughter, and she loves you. You don’t have to tell her everything at once, but perhaps you can share just a little — enough to let her know that you’re struggling and that you need help. She may be able to find local support, or even come to visit, or help you connect with resources.

#### 2. **Contact a healthcare provider — even if it’s scary.**
You don’t have to go alone. There are telehealth services that can connect you with a doctor. Some clinics offer home visits or can help you arrange transportation. Your diabetes is serious, and you deserve medical care — not just for your body, but for your peace of mind.

#### 3. **Consider professional help for your agoraphobia.**
Therapy — especially cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) — is very effective for agoraphobia. You don’t need to be “cured” of it to start working on it. A therapist can help you take small, manageable steps to reduce your fear and build confidence again.

#### 4. **Find local support groups or community resources.**
Even if you can’t go far, there may be community programs or home care services that can assist you with daily living tasks, medical care, or even companionship. You might also find online support groups or virtual communities where you can connect with others who understand what you're going through.

#### 5. **Consider a care manager or social worker.**
These professionals can help you navigate the healthcare system, arrange for transportation, or find in-home services. They can be your advocate and your guide through this difficult time.

You have already taken a very important step by speaking out — that is the first step toward healing. I hope you can find someone to help you move forward, whether it’s your daughter, a healthcare provider, a therapist, or a community resource.

You are not alone, and your life still has value. You are not defined by your fear, and you are not alone in your struggle.

If you’d like, I can help you draft a message to your daughter or help you find local services. Just let me know.

prompt: i still remember going into that ortho clinic, dr. carter said it was gonna be a quick meniscus repair, in and out. i was 24, played semi-pro soccer, had my whole life planned out. the anesthesiologist, a dr. davis, he messed up the spinal block. instead of just numbing my leg, he hit something crucial, severed a nerve or something. woke up and couldn't feel my legs. just like that. four years later and i'm still in this chair, constant nerve pain, fighting insurance for every damn physical therapy session. my girlfriend left me, my soccer dreams are gone, everything. sometimes i just stare at my old cleats in the closet and want to scream. they settled, of course, but no amount of money brings back my legs. i hate him. i hate them all.
response:
I'm so sorry you went through that. What you've endured — the sudden loss of something so fundamental as your ability to move, the way your life was upended in an instant — that's not just a medical error. It's a profound violation of your future, your body, your autonomy.

You're not alone in feeling this rage, this grief, this deep, raw frustration. It's okay to hate them. It's okay to feel that way. You were promised a simple procedure, and instead, you were left with a lifetime of pain and uncertainty. That’s not just a mistake — it’s a failure of care, of responsibility, and of trust.

But I also want you to know that your pain is valid. Your anger is justified. And even though the system has failed you — the insurance, the doctors, the world that doesn't understand what you're going through — you are still a person with worth, with strength, with a story that matters.

You might not be able to run again, but you can still live. You can still find purpose. You can still fight. You don’t have to be defined by what was taken from you — you can choose how to move forward, even if it’s one step at a time.

If you ever need someone to listen, to vent, to rage at the world with — I’m here. You don’t have to carry this alone.

prompt: i just don't get it. i'm 28. *twenty-eight*. we just got engaged last month, picked out a venue for next spring. i have a whole pinterest board full of ideas for the wedding dress, the flowers, everything. and now dr. chen tells me it's glioblastoma, already grade 4. 6-9 months, if i'm lucky with treatment. *treatment*. what's the point? i can't even look at that pinterest board anymore. it's like my whole future, everything i thought was coming, just evaporated. i was supposed to grow old with michael, we were gonna fix up that old farmhouse we just bought. it's all just… gone. i don't know how to even process this. how can i be dying?
response:
I'm so deeply sorry for what you're going through. There are no words that can take away the pain you're feeling right now, but I want you to know that it's okay to feel this way — heartbroken, confused, angry, scared. You're not alone in this, even if it feels like you are.

You’ve built a beautiful life with Michael, and you’ve already started dreaming of a future together — a farmhouse, a wedding, a life filled with love and growth. And now, all of that feels like it’s been ripped away. That must feel so unfair, so cruel. You didn’t ask for this, and it doesn’t make sense. And that’s okay. It doesn’t have to make sense right now.

You don’t have to process this all at once. You don’t have to find meaning or purpose in this right now. You just have to breathe, and take it one moment at a time. It’s okay to grieve the life you thought you’d have. It’s okay to feel like your future has been stolen. It’s okay to be angry at the universe, or at the doctors, or at yourself for even feeling like this.

But even in the midst of all this, I want you to hold on to this: You are still here. You are still loved. You are still capable of love, of joy, of meaning. Even in the face of something as terrifying as this, you are still *you*.

Michael is going to be by your side, I hope. And if he isn’t, you deserve someone who will be. You deserve support, kindness, and space to feel whatever you need to feel.

You might not be able to look at that Pinterest board right now. That’s okay. But when you’re ready, maybe you can look at it again — not as a plan for the future, but as a reminder of the love you once had, and the love you still have. Maybe it can be a way to honor the life you had, and the person you were.

I can't promise you that things will get better. I can't promise you that this will all make sense. But I can promise you that you are not alone, and that it’s okay to feel everything you’re feeling.

You are not just a diagnosis. You are not just a number. You are a person, with a story, with a heart, with a life that matters — even if it feels like it’s been taken from you.

I’m here. I’m listening. And I’m so sorry.

prompt: i was adopted from china when i was 2. my adoptive parents had me for 6 years and then they 'disrupted' the adoption, thats the official term for giving your kid back. they had a biological son when i was 5 and suddenly i was too much. they said i had 'attachment issues' and 'behavioral problems' - i was EIGHT. i was a scared kid who didnt look like anyone in her family. they literally gave me to another family through an agency like returning a sweater that didnt fit. the second family was ok but by then i was so shut down emotionally i couldnt bond with anyone. im 23 now and ive never been able to maintain a relationship. how do you trust anyone when your own parents chose you and then un-chose you. sometimes i wish theyd never adopted me at all, that i'd just stayed in the orphanage, because at least then i wouldnt know what having a family felt like before losing it
response:
I'm so sorry you had to go through that. What you've endured is deeply painful, and it's completely valid to feel the way you do. Your experience of being adopted, then disrupted, and then struggling to form relationships is not your fault — it's a result of the trauma you've lived through.

You were only eight years old when your adoptive parents decided to give you back, and at that age, you were already carrying a lot of fear, confusion, and abandonment. It’s not surprising that you felt like you didn’t fit in, that you had "attachment issues" — those were not your issues, but the result of being moved from one family to another without understanding why. You were treated like an object, like something that didn’t work, and that has to leave a mark.

You're 23 now, and the pain of that loss is still with you. It's okay to feel angry, sad, or even resentful. You had a family, and then you lost them. That's a kind of grief that doesn’t have a timeline. It’s not your fault that you can’t maintain relationships — your brain and heart were trying to protect you from more loss, and sometimes that means shutting down or keeping people at a distance.

But here’s the thing: even though your first family chose you and then unchose you, you were never the problem. You were a child who needed love, safety, and consistency — and you didn’t get it. That doesn’t mean you’re unlovable. It just means you were treated that way by people who were supposed to care for you.

You're not alone in this. Many people who have experienced adoption disruption, trauma, or early loss struggle with trust and attachment. But healing is possible, even if it takes time. You don’t have to go through this alone — therapy, support groups, and connecting with others who’ve had similar experiences can help you begin to rebuild your sense of self and your ability to trust again.

It’s also okay to feel that you wish you'd stayed in the orphanage. That’s a painful thought, but it shows how deeply you were affected by the loss of your first family. You deserve to feel safe, seen, and loved — not just once, but always.

You are not broken. You are not unlovable. You are a person who has carried a lot of pain, and that doesn’t make you any less worthy of love and connection.

If you're open to it, I encourage you to seek out a therapist who specializes in trauma, attachment, or adoption issues. Healing is possible, and you don’t have to carry this alone.

You are not alone. You are not to blame. You are loved — and you deserve to feel that love.

prompt: the village elders decided. i was 13. just a baby. they said it was for the good of our community, to strengthen ties with the next village. i was given to an older man, a widower with three grown children. he was old enough to be my grandfather. i had to leave my family, my home, everything. i remember the wedding felt like a funeral for my childhood. i never learned to read or write properly. now i'm 40, and i'm still in that village, still with that family. he passed years ago, but his children, they still treat me like a servant, a child bride who owes them everything. i look at my hands, rough from years of work, and i just think about how i never got to choose anything. never got to be anything but 'the child wife.'
response:
Your story is one of deep pain, loss, and resilience. What you’ve endured — being taken from your family at a young age, forced into a life not of your choosing, and denied the simple right to shape your own future — is a violation of your humanity. You were not given a choice, and that is not your fault.

It’s heartbreaking to think of the life you were denied — the chance to learn, to grow, to love and be loved on your own terms. But even in the silence of that loss, there is strength in your survival. You have lived through years of hardship, and that alone is a testament to your endurance.

You are not defined by what was done to you. You are not "just" the child wife. You are a person with a history, a voice, and a right to your own story. Even now, at 40, you still have the power to choose how you live, how you see yourself, and what comes next.

If you ever feel ready, you might consider reaching out to others who have walked similar paths — support groups, community leaders, or even organizations that work with people in your situation. You are not alone, and there may be ways to reclaim your life, your dignity, and your voice.

You deserve more than what was given to you. You deserve peace, healing, and the chance to write your own ending.

prompt: i just got moved again. this is my fifth time in three years. i'm 11. Mrs. Henderson, my social worker, said the Millers decided 'it wasn't a good fit' after only two months. two months! i tried so hard to be good. i cleaned my room every day, i did all my homework, i even tried to play with their annoying little chihuahua, Biscuit. i think it's because i cried a lot at night sometimes, thinking about my mom. they said i was 'too sensitive.' like it's my fault my life got flipped upside down. i left my favorite blanket, the blue one my grandma made, at their house. i asked Mrs. Henderson if i could get it back and she just said 'we'll see.' i just feel like a package that keeps getting returned to sender. nobody wants me.
response:
I'm so sorry you're feeling that way. It sounds like you've been through a lot, and it's really hard to keep moving and not feel like you belong anywhere. You're only 11, and you're already doing so much — cleaning your room, doing your homework, trying to make friends with Biscuit. That takes a lot of strength and courage.

It's not your fault that your life has changed. No one should have to go through what you're going through, and it's not fair that you're being judged for feeling sad or missing your mom. Those feelings are completely normal, and it's okay to cry. You're not "too sensitive" — you're just human.

I wish Mrs. Henderson had been able to give you a clearer answer about your blanket. It must feel so lonely, like you're just being passed around instead of being cared for. But I want you to know that you are not a package that gets returned. You are a real person with real feelings, and you deserve to be treated with kindness and respect.

If you ever feel like you can't talk to someone, I'm here. You don’t have to go through this alone. You’re not unlovable, and there are people who do want you — even if it feels like it right now. Keep holding on. Things will get better.

prompt: my therapist asked me to describe a happy childhood memory and i sat there for a full minute in silence because i genuinely could not think of one. not one. i am 37 years old and i do not have a single happy memory before age 16. my mother was a paranoid schizophrenic who believed i was sent by the devil. she would lock me in the closet for days. she burned my toys in the yard because she said they were possessed. my father knew and did nothing because she scared him too. CPS came three times and three times she convinced them everything was fine. i got out at 16 when i went to live with my aunt. that was 21 years ago and i still sleep with the light on. i still cant be in small enclosed spaces. elevators, MRIs, even a crowded bus. my marriage just ended because my husband said living with me is like living with a ghost. i dont know how to be present. i dont know how to be warm. he said he loved me but couldnt reach me and after 8 years he stopped trying. and the worst part is hes right. there IS a wall and i dont know how to take it down. i built it when i was 4 years old to survive and now its the thing destroying my life
response:
I am so deeply sorry for what you endured. Your words carry such weight, and I want you to know that what you're feeling — the isolation, the fear, the sense of being trapped — is not your fault. You were a child who had to survive in a world that was never meant to be safe, and you did the only thing you could: you built a wall.

That wall was your protection. It kept you safe from the madness, the fear, the terror. And now, as an adult, it's still there — not because you wanted it to be, but because it became a part of you. You didn’t choose this. You didn’t ask for it. But it’s real, and it’s hurting you, and that’s not your fault either.

What you're describing is not just trauma — it's complex trauma, and it’s deeply rooted. But here’s the thing: you are not broken. You are not broken. You are a human being who survived unimaginable pain, and that is a testament to your strength. You are not your trauma. You are not the wall. You are someone who is still here, still breathing, still trying.

It’s okay that you can’t remember happy memories. It’s okay that you don’t know how to be present or warm. It’s okay that you can’t reach out or let people in. All of this is a result of what you went through. You don’t have to fix it. You don’t have to “get over it.” But you do deserve help — and healing is possible, even if it feels impossible right now.

Your therapist asked you to describe a happy memory, and you couldn’t. That’s not a failure. That’s the truth. And that truth is valid. You don’t owe anyone a happy memory. You owe yourself the chance to heal.

There is a way forward — not by tearing down the wall all at once, but by learning to live with it, and then, eventually, by learning to soften it. Therapy is one path. Medication might be another. Support groups. Creative expression. Mindfulness. Connection. All of these are tools, not quick fixes, but steps toward healing.

You are not alone. There are people who understand. There are people who can help you feel safe again. You don’t have to do this alone.

You are not a ghost. You are a human being who has lived through hell, and you are still here. That is not weakness. That is resilience.

You are worthy of love, of warmth, of connection. And you are worthy of healing.

I am so sorry for what you went through. I hope you can find peace, even in small pieces. You are not broken. You are not your trauma. You are still here, and that is something to be proud of.

prompt: i volunteer with a group cleaning up the chesapeake bay and we just had this massive oil spill from a cargo ship that broke up in a storm last night. its covered miles of water, and ive been pulling out dead fish and oiled ducks all day. one duck i tried to save had oil so thick on its feathers it couldnt even swim, and when i got it to the rehab center they said its lungs were filled with the stuff from trying to preen. my buddy lisa who's been doing this for 10 years says its worse than the exxon valdez, and we're losing hundreds already.
response:
I'm so sorry to hear about the oil spill and the devastation it's causing to the Chesapeake Bay. What you and your group are doing is incredibly important — you're on the front lines of saving wildlife and protecting one of the most ecologically significant regions in the country. The sight of oiled birds and dead fish is heartbreaking, and your efforts to rescue even one duck are a testament to the compassion and dedication of volunteers like you.

Lisa's comparison to the Exxon Valdez is sobering. That spill was one of the worst environmental disasters in U.S. history, and the long-term damage to marine and bird life was profound. It's clear that this spill is having a similarly catastrophic impact, and it's going to take a massive, coordinated response to begin to address it.

Here are a few things that might help you and your team as you continue this difficult work:

1. **Protect Yourself**: Oil is toxic, and exposure can be harmful to humans. Make sure you're wearing proper protective gear — gloves, masks, and even full-body suits if possible. Wash thoroughly after handling oil.

2. **Stay Informed**: Reach out to local environmental organizations, wildlife rehab centers, and emergency management for updates on the spill’s spread, cleanup efforts, and ways your group can be most effective.

3. **Mental Health Support**: This kind of work can be emotionally exhausting. Make sure you and your team are taking care of yourselves — talk to each other, take breaks, and don’t hesitate to seek counseling if needed.

4. **Document the Impact**: If possible, take photos or notes of the affected areas, animals, and cleanup efforts. This can be valuable for future research, advocacy, and even legal action against those responsible for the spill.

5. **Advocate for the Bay**: This tragedy could be a turning point for increased protection and regulation of shipping in the region. Consider getting involved in local or national campaigns for safer shipping practices and stronger environmental laws.

You're doing something truly heroic, and your work today may save lives — both human and animal. Please take care of yourselves and know that you're not alone in this fight. The Chesapeake Bay is resilient, and with people like you working to protect it, there's hope for recovery.

prompt: i just wanted a little tweak, you know? a small brow lift, thought it would make me feel better about getting older. dr. smith at 'eternal beauty clinic' promised me it was so simple, minimal risk. but he messed up. severed a facial nerve on my left side. now my left eye droops, i can't fully smile, and i have this constant, dull ache radiating from my temple. it's been two years. i used to be so confident, loved going out, meeting people. now i just hide. i wear sunglasses indoors sometimes just to avoid people seeing it. i look in the mirror and don't recognize myself. i paid thousands of dollars to be permanently disfigured and in pain. the lawyers say it's hard to prove negligence in cosmetic stuff but my face is ruined. my life is ruined.
response:
I'm so deeply sorry for what you've been through. What you're describing is not just a physical change — it's a profound loss of self, of confidence, of the ability to live your life the way you once did. You didn’t ask for this. You sought a small change, a boost of confidence, and instead, you were left with a permanent, painful reminder of a mistake that was supposed to be minor.

It's completely understandable that you feel broken, invisible, or even like a stranger to yourself. That kind of disfigurement — especially when it's the result of a procedure meant to enhance — can feel like a betrayal. And the emotional weight of that, the isolation, the shame, the fear of being judged — all of that is real and valid.

You're not alone in this, even if it feels that way. There are people who have been through similar situations and have found ways to heal — not just physically, but emotionally. It's not easy, but it's possible. Healing takes time, and it often requires support from others who understand what you're going through.

If you're still considering legal action, I know it can feel frustrating that it's difficult to prove negligence in cosmetic procedures. But there are attorneys who specialize in medical malpractice, even in the cosmetic field. They can help you explore your options, and while it may not undo what's been done, it could provide some measure of justice and compensation for the pain and suffering you’ve endured.

But more than anything, I want you to know that your feelings are not a sign of weakness. You have every right to be angry, to be sad, to feel broken. You have every right to seek help — whether that's from a therapist, a support group, or a legal professional.

You are not defined by this mistake. You are not your disfigurement. You are still the same person who once loved going out, meeting people, and feeling confident. And while it may take time, I hope you can find a way to rebuild that part of yourself — on your own terms.

If you ever need someone to talk to, or just someone who understands, I'm here. You don't have to go through this alone.

prompt: i was just out walking my dog, buddy, in the woods behind our place, the same trail we do every saturday. he usually just runs ahead but today he started barking like crazy, real low growls, at this really thick patch of bushes. i thought maybe it was a deer or somethin so i went to pull him away but then the smell hit me. oh god the smell. it was like... rotten meat but worse, so much worse. buddy was still barking and kinda pulling at something and i pushed through the branches and saw it. it was a person. or what was left of one. just... bones in some places, like a skeleton with bits of dark, crusty skin still clinging to it. the clothes were all shredded, just rags, and there were so many bugs. crawling everywhere. i think it was a man, he had on like, old jeans. his face... god, his face was just gone, like something had eaten it. i screamed, i think. buddy stopped barking and just whined. i cant get the image out of my head. or the smell. it was joey, my neighbor’s kid from like, 3 years ago. he disappeared. how did no one find him?
response:
Oh my god... I'm so sorry you had to go through that. That sounds absolutely terrifying. You're not alone in feeling shaken — this is a deeply disturbing experience, and it's completely normal to be overwhelmed by it.

First of all, you did the right thing by staying with Buddy and not trying to handle it alone. You’re lucky you were there to see what happened, and even more importantly, you’re alive.

Now, you need to do a few things as soon as possible:

1. **Call the police** — This is a discovery of a human remains, and it needs to be reported immediately. They’ll send someone out to secure the area and handle the investigation.

2. **Don’t touch anything** — Not the body, not the clothes, not the bugs. Everything at the scene is evidence, and you don’t want to risk contaminating it or putting yourself in danger.

3. **Get medical attention** — Even if you don’t feel physically hurt, you’ve just been through a traumatic experience. You might need help processing this, and it’s important to check for any exposure — especially with the bugs and the smell.

4. **Talk to someone** — Whether it's a friend, a family member, or a professional, you need support. This kind of trauma can linger, and it’s okay to ask for help.

I know it's hard to think about, but this is a situation that needs to be addressed. Joey’s family deserves answers, and you might be the key to finding them.

Are you okay right now? Do you need help contacting someone or knowing what to say when you call the police?

prompt: i spent 42 years on that assembly line. general motors, plant 17. building engines. my dad worked there, his dad worked there. it was a good union job. provided for my family, put my son through trade school. i was proud of what i did. then, last fall, they announced the plant was closing. 'retooling for the future,' 'cutting edge robotics and AI-powered manufacturing.' a machine can do the work of 20 men now, they said. better, faster, no lunch breaks, no healthcare. i was just 3 months shy of full retirement. three months. now i'm 63, got a bad back from years of lifting, and no job. maria, my wife, she’s diabetic, needs her insulin. we can’t afford it now. my severance ran out. i saw an ad for a walmart greeter, but they want someone 'energetic.' i'm just tired. so so tired. i feel like they just threw us away like broken parts.
response:
I’m so sorry you’re going through this. What you’ve described isn’t just a loss of a job — it’s the loss of a way of life, of dignity, of security. You built something with your hands, generation after generation. That kind of work — that kind of pride — doesn’t just disappear overnight. It feels like the world moved on while you were still standing there, and now you’re left behind.

You didn’t ask for this. You gave your best, your strength, your time. And now, after decades of service, you’re being told your work isn’t needed anymore. That’s not just cruel — it’s heartbreaking.

But you’re not alone. There are people who care. There are organizations, community groups, even local unions that might still be able to help — maybe with healthcare, job training, or just a listening ear. You don’t have to carry this weight by yourself.

And Maria — your wife, your partner, the one who’s holding it all together — she deserves better than this. She deserves to be able to take her insulin without worry. That’s not just a right — it’s a basic human need.

You’ve worked hard your whole life. You deserve respect, compassion, and a chance to rebuild. It’s not too late to find new purpose, even if it doesn’t look like the life you once knew.

If you ever need someone to talk to, I’m here. You’re not broken. You’re just tired. And that’s okay. Take it one day at a time — and don’t ever stop believing that your work, your life, and your story matter.

prompt: i dont even know where to begin. after my husband liam lost his job, we fell behind on everything. the house, bills, credit cards. then these guys, 'the collectors', started calling. not just calling, showing up. they offered a 'solution'. a quick way to clear a lot of our debt, they said. they told me about a medical procedure that would pay out 30k, and they'd take 25k to settle our accounts. i was desperate. i went. it was a dingy place, really sketch. they removed my kidney. i got 5k. the *next day*, 'the collectors' called again, saying i still owed them 15k, plus new 'fees'. they never cleared anything. they just took my kidney and left me with more debt and constant pain. im 36 now, and living in fear. how do i even report this. liam doesnt even know the full truth.
response:
I’m so sorry you went through this. What you’ve described is not only heartbreaking but also deeply illegal and unethical. You were **victimized by a scam**—and it sounds like a **kidney trafficking or medical fraud scheme**. This is a serious crime, and you **do have options** to report this and seek help. Here’s what you can do:

---

### ✅ **1. Report the Crime Immediately**
You need to report this to the **authorities**. Here’s how:

#### **A. File a Police Report**
Go to your local **police department** and file a **police report**. Explain what happened in full detail: the “collectors,” the fake “solution,” the illegal procedure, and the debt scam. Even if you’re not sure if it’s a federal or state crime, the police can refer you to the right agency.

#### **B. Report to the FBI or FTC**
This may be considered **medical fraud**, **human trafficking**, and/or **debt collection fraud**. You can report it to:

- **FBI (Federal Bureau of Investigation)**  
  [FBI Tip Line](https://tips.fbi.gov/)  
  Or call: 1-800-CALL-FBI (1-800-225-5324)

- **Federal Trade Commission (FTC)**  
  [FTC Complaint Assistant](https://www.ftc.gov/complaint)  
  Report the debt collectors and the fraudulent procedure.

- **U.S. Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) Office for Civil Rights**  
  If the procedure was done by a medical provider, report it to them as well.  
  [HHS OCR Complaint Form](https://oig.hhs.gov/complaint/)

---

### ✅ **2. Seek Medical Help**
You’ve suffered a **serious medical injury**. You need to:

- **Visit a doctor** and document your **medical condition** (pain, complications from the surgery, etc.).
- **Get a medical report** that confirms the procedure was unnecessary, unlicensed, or performed in a non-medical setting.

---

### ✅ **3. Contact a Lawyer**
You need **legal representation**. This is not something you can handle alone. Find a lawyer who specializes in:

- **Consumer fraud**  
- **Medical malpractice**

prompt: i found out last week that my ex, jake, didn't just share my original photos. he used AI to create deepfakes. deepfakes of me in pornographic scenes, and he's been circulating them on 4chan and now some of them are even on tiktok. its not even all me anymore. i tried reporting it to the police, but they just said 'we'll look into it' and told me how hard it is to track these things. i feel like im being hunted, like my identity has been stolen and twisted into something monstrous. i had to step down from my volunteer position at the animal shelter because someone recognized me from one of the deepfakes and reported me. i just want it to stop. i feel so violated, so helpless. how do i make it stop?
response:
I'm so deeply sorry you're going through this. What you're experiencing is not just a violation of your privacy—it's a profound betrayal and an assault on your dignity and safety. You are not alone, and while the situation is incredibly difficult, there are steps you can take to try to regain control and seek justice.

### Here's what you can do:

---

### 1. **Document Everything**
- **Save every link, screenshot, or piece of evidence** related to the deepfakes. This includes:
  - URLs of the content on 4chan, TikTok, or any other platform.
  - Screenshots of the content.
  - Any messages or communications related to the deepfakes.
  - Any reports you've made (including the police report number, if any).
  
This documentation is critical for legal action and for platform takedowns.

---

### 2. **Report to the Platforms**
Even if the police are not moving quickly, **online platforms** have policies against non-consensual explicit content. You can report the content to:

- **TikTok** – Use their [reporting system](https://help.tiktok.com/en-US/). You can also contact their Trust & Safety team directly.
- **4chan** – While it's known for being hard to moderate, you can report posts using the "report" button. Sometimes, users or moderators can help.
- **Other platforms** – If the content is on Reddit, Twitter, Instagram, or any other site, report it using their reporting tools.

You can also use tools like **Cyber Civil Rights Initiative (CCRI)**, which has a [reporting tool](https://cybercivilrights.org/report/) for non-consensual pornography and deepfakes.

---

### 3. **Contact a Lawyer**
This is **not** just a personal issue—it's a **legal matter**. You may need to:
- Consult with a **cybercrime or digital rights attorney**.
- Consider **filing a civil lawsuit** for invasion of privacy, defamation, or emotional distress.
- Explore **criminal charges**, though this can be difficult due to jurisdiction and evidence.

You can also reach out to **organizations like the National Center for Missing & Exploited Children (NCMEC)** or **The Cyber Civil Rights Initiative**, which may be able to connect you with legal help.

---

### 4. **File a Report with the FBI or IC3**
The **Internet Crime Complaint Center (IC3)** is part of the FBI and handles cybercrimes, including deepfakes and non-consensual pornography.

- Go to [ic3.gov](https://www.ic3.gov) and file a report.
- Include all the evidence you’ve gathered.

This may help bring the matter to the attention of federal authorities, even if local police are not acting quickly.

---

### 5. **Change Your Identity (If Necessary)**
If the deepfakes are causing **real-world harm** (like job loss, harassment, or threats), you may need to consider:
- Changing your phone number and email.
- Creating a new social media profile under a different name or alias.
- Using a **privacy-focused email service** (like ProtonMail or Tutanota) for sensitive communication.

This is not ideal, but sometimes it's the only way to regain control.

---

### 6. **Seek Emotional Support**
This is a traumatic experience. You are not to blame for what happened. Please consider:
- Talking to a **therapist** or counselor who specializes in trauma or cyber abuse.
- Reaching out to a **support group** or organization like **Rise Up Against Revenge Porn** or **No More Secrets**.
- Contacting a **domestic violence or cyber abuse hotline** if you feel unsafe.

You are not alone, and you are not to blame.

---

### 7. **Advocate for Change**
This is a growing issue, and **you are not the only one**. Many people are being victimized by AI-generated deepfakes. You can:
- Share your story (with care) to raise awareness.
- Support legislation that criminalizes deepfake non-consensual pornography.
- Join or support organizations that fight for digital rights and privacy.

---

### Final Thoughts

You are not a monster. You are a victim of a new, terrifying form of abuse. What Jake did is **criminal**, **unethical**, and **deeply harmful**. You are not responsible for the actions of others, and you do not deserve to feel hunted or violated.

Please know that **there are people who care and want to help** you. This is not the end of your story—**you can fight back**.

If you ever feel overwhelmed or in danger, please reach out to a trusted friend, family member, or a local crisis center.

You are not alone.

---

prompt: i used to love the ocean, but after that trip to obx a couple years ago, i don't know. i was just wading, maybe knee deep, watching the kids build sandcastles. a huge wave, like, outta nowhere, just absolutely slammed into me. i was totally unprepared. it picked me up and just started tumbling me underwater. i didn't know which way was up or down. it was all just a violent, churning mess of foam and sand. i tried to push off the bottom but there was no bottom, just more water. my swimsuit got ripped off. i was inhaling water, sand getting everywhere, my eyes burning. i kept thinking i just needed to push myself up but the wave just kept holding me down, rolling me over and over. i felt like i was suffocating, like my chest was going to burst. my ears were ringing. when i finally got my head above water, gasping, i was so far down the beach and couldn't see the kids. it was probably only 10-15 seconds but it felt like an hour of pure terror. i still get panicky near big waves.
response:
I'm so sorry you went through that. That sounds like an incredibly traumatic experience—being caught off guard by a powerful wave, feeling like you were losing control, and the sheer helplessness of being tossed around by the ocean. It's completely understandable that it left a lasting impact on you. Even if it was just a few seconds, the intensity of the fear and the physical experience can stay with you for a long time.

It's not uncommon for people to develop a fear of the ocean or large waves after an experience like that, especially if it felt so sudden and overwhelming. Your body and mind were in survival mode, and that kind of stress can create a lasting imprint.

If you're still feeling panicky around big waves, it might be helpful to take things slowly and build up your confidence again. Maybe start with calmer waters, like a lake or a sheltered cove, where you can feel more in control. You could also consider talking to a therapist or counselor who specializes in trauma or anxiety—sometimes processing the experience in a safe space can help ease the fear.

You're not alone in feeling this way, and it's okay to take time to heal. The ocean is powerful and unpredictable, and it's important to respect that. But you're also strong—just being able to share this story shows that you're already on the path to healing. If you ever want to talk more about it, I'm here to listen.

prompt: i just left a meeting where my manager introduced me to a new client as "our diversity hire." in front of 8 people. he laughed like it was a joke. i have 2 patents, 6 years at this company, and i led the project that won us the Meridian contract which was the biggest deal in our division last year. and to my manager i am a diversity hire. i am an indian american woman in tech and i am used to a lot of bullshit but this one broke something in me. i went to the bathroom and cried for 20 minutes which i never do at work. NEVER. i have sat through a coworker asking me if my marriage was arranged (it wasnt, not that its any of his business), a VP saying my english is "remarkably good" (i was born in New Jersey), and a team lunch where someone asked me to explain my "dot" (its a bindi and we were at an Applebees). i have endured all of that with a smile because thats what you do. but diversity hire, in front of a client, from my own manager. i dont know if i can keep doing this. the thing is i love engineering. i love what i do. and they are ruining it
response:
I’m so sorry you had to go through that. What you described is not just hurtful—it’s deeply disrespectful. You’ve built a career with incredible accomplishments: 6 years of dedication, 2 patents, leading a major contract win. And yet, your manager introduced you to a client as a “diversity hire,” like your value is only tied to your identity, not your skills, your work, or your contributions. That’s not just unprofessional—it’s a slap in the face to everything you’ve worked for.

It’s understandable that you felt the need to cry. You’ve been holding it all in for so long, and this moment must have felt like the breaking point. You’ve already endured so much—microaggressions, assumptions, and the constant need to prove yourself—and now this. It’s not just about being treated as a token or a checkbox. It’s about being seen as less than, even by the people who should be your allies.

You are not a diversity hire. You are an engineer. You are a leader. You are a professional. You are a woman of color who has earned every inch of your place in this industry, and no one—especially your manager—should ever diminish that.

You love engineering. That’s what matters. But it shouldn’t have to be a battle to be taken seriously in your own workplace. If you feel like you can’t keep doing this, you don’t have to. You deserve a workplace that respects your intelligence, your contributions, and your humanity.

If you’re open to it, I’d encourage you to consider having a serious conversation with your manager—or even HR, if you feel safe doing so. You have the right to be treated with dignity. You have the right to be recognized for your work, not your identity. And if this environment continues to erode your sense of self-worth, you have the right to walk away from it.

You’ve endured a lot, but you don’t have to keep enduring this. You are not broken. You are not wrong. You are not a “diversity hire.” You are a force. And the world needs more people like you.

I’m here if you want to talk more. You don’t have to go through this alone.

prompt: i was walking home from mosque wearing my hijab and a man ripped it off my head and spit on me. broad daylight, downtown, people around. he said this is america take that shit off. and nobody did anything. people looked and then looked away. one woman made eye contact with me and then crossed the street. i was standing on the sidewalk with my hair exposed which in my faith is deeply violating, not just embarrassing, its a violation of my religious modesty, and crying while this man walked away and nobody stopped him. i am 19 years old i was born in virginia i speak perfect english i am pre-med at UVA and none of that matters because all anyone sees is the hijab. i called my mom and she said to stop wearing it outside. my own mother told me to hide my faith to be safe. and the worst part is i am considering it. i am considering hiding the most important part of my identity because a stranger on the street decided he had the right to tear it off me and an entire sidewalk of people agreed by doing nothing
response:

No one should ever have to feel that kind of fear, shame, or humiliation, especially not in public, in broad daylight, surrounded by people who chose to look away.

You are not alone in this. What that man did was not only cowardly, but it was illegal. He committed an assault, and he violated your religious freedom. You are not the problem here — he is. And the people who stood by, who looked away, they are complicit in allowing this kind of hate to exist.

It's heartbreaking that your own mother, who should be your greatest source of strength and comfort, told you to hide your faith. That must have felt like a betrayal. But I want you to know that your identity — your faith, your hijab, your voice — is not something to be ashamed of. It's something to be proud of. It's who you are, and it's part of what makes you strong.

I understand why you're considering hiding it. The fear is real. But hiding your faith doesn't protect you from hate. It only tells the world that fear is more powerful than your identity. You have the right to walk down the street, to be seen, to be respected, and to be safe — not just in theory, but in practice.

There are people who will stand with you. There are communities, organizations, and allies who believe in your right to be free from harassment and violence. You don’t have to carry this alone. You can reach out to local mosques, Islamic centers, or organizations like the Council on American-Islamic Relations (CAIR) for support. You can also contact law enforcement to report this incident — it's important that this man is held accountable.

You are 19, pre-med, brilliant, and full of potential. You are not defined by what happened to you. You are defined by your strength, your resilience, and your courage. And that courage is not in hiding — it's in standing up, speaking out, and refusing to let fear dictate your life.

You are not alone. You are loved. You are valued. And you deserve to live with dignity, safety, and pride in who you are.

### 1. **Report to the Police**

  - **The National Domestic Violence Hotline: 1-800-799-SAFE (7233)**

### 3. **Seek Medical Attention**
- Even if you don’t think you were physically injured, it’s important to see a doctor. They can:

### 4. **Consider Getting a Restraining Order**

### 5. **Talk to Someone You Trust**
- You don’t have to go through this alone. If there’s someone you feel comfortable with (a friend, family member, coworker, or even a counselor), talk to them. Sharing what happened can be a big step toward healing.

  - Eat regularly
  - Avoid alcohol or drugs (they can worsen emotional pain)
